question: A 67-year-old woman with a history of congenital bicuspid aortic valve is hospitalized with a 2-day history of fever and chills. Her only medication is lisinopril. On examination, her temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F) and cardiac auscultation reveals a grade 3/6 systolic murmur loudest at the second right intercostal space. Blood cultures are positive for penicillin-susceptible viridans streptococci. A second antibiotic, which acts synergistically with penicillin, is added to her regimen to potentially reduce the length of therapy. What is the most likely mechanism by which this second antibiotic exerts its effect on the bacteria?
original_question:
A 67-year-old woman with congenital bicuspid aortic valve is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-day history of
fever and chills. Current medication is lisinopril. Temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are
20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a grade 3/6 systolic murmur that is best
heard over the second right intercostal space. Blood culture grows viridans streptococci susceptible to penicillin. In
addition to penicillin, an antibiotic synergistic to penicillin is administered that may help shorten the duration of this
patient's drug treatment. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of action of this additional antibiotic
on bacteria?

question: A 12-year-old girl presents for evaluation of jaundice that has occurred intermittently over the past two months. On examination, jaundice is the only abnormal finding. Laboratory results reveal a total bilirubin of 3 mg/dL, with a direct bilirubin of 1 mg/dL. Serum haptoglobin, AST, and ALT levels are all normal. There is no history of trauma or toxic exposures. Which of the following is the most likely associated finding in this patient?
original_question:
A 12-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of a 2-month history of intermittent yellowing of the eyes and
skin. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for jaundice. Her serum total bilirubin concentration is
3 mg/dL, with a direct component of 1 mg/dL. Serum studies show a haptoglobin concentration and AST and ALT
activities that are within the reference ranges. There is no evidence of injury or exposure to toxins. Which of the
following additional findings is most likely in this patient?

question:
A guinea pig intestinal smooth muscle strip is treated with drug X in a muscle bath experiment. The following changes in muscle tension are observed after administration of various agonists:

Agonist: Vehicle; Tension before drug X: 6.0 g; Tension after drug X: 6.1 g
Agonist: Acetylcholine; Tension before drug X: 11.3 g; Tension after drug X: 18.5 g
Agonist: Norepinephrine; Tension before drug X: 4.1 g; Tension after drug X: 4.2 g

Based on these data, the effects of drug X are most similar to which class of pharmacological agents?
original_question:
During an experiment, drug X is added to a muscle bath containing a strip of guinea pig intestinal smooth muscle.
Agonists are added to the bath, and the resultant effects on muscle tension are shown in the table.
Agonist  Muscle Tension Before Drug X (g)  Muscle Tension After Drug X (g)  
Vehicle  6.0 6.1 
Acetylcholine  11.3 18.5 
Norepinephrine  4.1 4.2 
Which of the following types of drugs is most likely to produce effects most similar to those of drug X?

question: A 30-year-old woman presents for a routine prenatal visit at 28 weeks of gestation. Her obstetric history is significant for one prior spontaneous abortion at 12 weeks. She is currently gravida 2, para 0, aborta 1. Vital signs and uterine size are normal for gestational age. Fetal ultrasound reveals a normal male fetus. Maternal blood typing is O, Rh-negative, while the father is B, Rh-positive. Based on this information, administration of which agent is indicated?

options.D: Generation of IgM antibodies from fixing complement in the fetus
options.E: Immunosuppression caused by RhD on erythrocytes from the fetus

original_question:
A 30-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 0, aborta 1, at 28 weeks' gestation comes to the office for a prenatal visit. She
has had one previous pregnancy resulting in a spontaneous abortion at 12 weeks' gestation. Today, her vital signs are
within normal limits. Physical examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 28-week gestation. Fetal
ultrasonography shows a male fetus with no abnormalities. Her blood group is O, Rh-negative. The father's blood
group is B, Rh-positive. The physician recommends administration of Rh o

question: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension and end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of dyspnea and altered mental status. An electrocardiogram reveals low voltage and electrical alternans. Which of the following findings is most likely to be present on his physical examination?
original_question:
A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of shortness of breath and confusion for
4 hours. He has hypertension and chronic kidney disease requiring hemodialysis. An ECG shows low voltage with
electrical alternans. Physical examination is most likely to show which of the following findings?
Blood Pressure  Jugular Venous  Pulsus  
(mm Hg)  Pulse (/min)  Pressure  Paradoxus

question: A 52-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus starts a medication. After one month of treatment, laboratory studies indicate a reduction in hepatic glucose production and an enhancement of glucose uptake and use in peripheral tissues. Which medication was most likely initiated?
original_question:
A 52-year-old woman begins pharmacotherapy after being diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus. Four weeks
later, her hepatic glucose output is decreased, and target tissue glucose uptake and utilization are increased. Which
of the following drugs was most likely prescribed for this patient?

question:
A 44-year-old man with no symptoms is diagnosed with HIV infection discovered during a pre-donation blood screening. Laboratory studies from the screening visit are as follows:
Hemoglobin: 10 g/dL
Hematocrit: 30%
Leukocyte count: 4600/mm³
Platelet count: 15,000/mm³
Prothrombin time: 12 sec (INR 1.1)
Partial thromboplastin time: 23 sec
Which of the following physical examination findings is this patient most likely to exhibit?
original_question:
An asymptomatic 44-year-old man is found to have HIV infection during routine screening prior to donating blood.
A complete blood count done at the time of the screening shows:
Hemoglobin  10 g/dL  
Hematocrit  30% 
Leukocyte count  4600/mm3 
Platelet count  15,000/mm3 
Prothrombin time  12 sec (INR=1.1)  
Partial thromboplastin time  23 sec  
Which of the following physical findings is most likely in this patient?

question: A 23-year-old female with aplastic anemia receives a high-dose course of rabbit antithymocyte globulin. Approximately ten days following treatment, she presents with fever, swollen lymph nodes, joint pain, and a rash on her palms and soles. What is the most probable etiology for this clinical picture?

options.A: Cytokine secretion by natural killer cells

original_question:
A 23-year-old woman with bone marrow failure is treated with a large dose of rabbit antithymocyte globulin. Ten
days later, she develops fever, lymphadenopathy, arthralgias, and erythema on her hands and feet. Which of the
following is the most likely cause of these symptoms?

question: A 42-year-old woman undergoes multiple surgical procedures, including repair of a perforated small bowel, facial fractures, and a left femur fracture following major trauma. Thirty-six hours after surgery, while awake but not fully alert, she expresses concern about developing an addiction to the morphine she is receiving via a patient-controlled pump for pain, despite having no history of substance use. Her alcohol intake is minimal. What is the most suitable initial step for the physician to take?

options.A: Reassure the patient that her chance of becoming addicted to narcotics is minuscule
options.B: Maintain the morphine, but periodically administer intravenous naloxone
options.C: Switch the patient to oral acetaminophen as soon as she can take medication orally
options.D: Switch the patient to intramuscular lorazepam
options.E: Switch the patient to intravenous phenobarbital

answer: Reassure the patient that her chance of becoming addicted to narcotics is minuscule
original_question:
After being severely beaten and sustaining a gunshot wound to the abdomen, a 42-year-old woman undergoes
resection of a perforated small bowel. During the operation, plastic reconstruction of facial fractures, and open
reduction and internal fixation of the left femur are also done. Thirty-six hours postoperatively, she is awake but not
completely alert. She is receiving intravenous morphine via a patient-controlled pump. She says that she needs the
morphine to treat her pain, but she is worried that she is becoming addicted. She has no history of substance use
disorder. She drinks one to two glasses of wine weekly. Which of the following initial actions by the physician is
most appropriate?

question: A 22-year-old healthy woman presents with a 4-day history of a pruritic, erythematous rash on her right arm. She reports no significant medical history or current medications but notes recent use of a new hair dye. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination of the affected forearm reveals the findings shown. Which ligand-receptor interaction was most critical for the proliferation of T lymphocytes at the site of this rash?

options.A: CD2 on T lymphocytes → inte rcellular adhesion molecule (ICAM)-3 on epidermal Langerhans cells
options.B: CD28 on T lymphocytes → CD80 on epidermal Langerhans cells
options.C: CD40L on T lymphocytes → CD40 on epidermal Langerhans cells
options.D: CD45 on T lymphocytes → CD28 on epidermal Langerhans cells
options.E: Lymphocyte function-associated antigen- 1 on T lymphocytes → ICAM1 on epidermal Langerhans cells

answer: CD28 on T lymphocytes → CD80 on epidermal Langerhans cells
original_question:
A 22-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 4-day history of an itchy, red rash on her right arm. She has
no history of major medical illness and takes no medications. She says she has recently used a new hair dye. She is
165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 61 kg (135 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is
70/min. respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Examination of the right forearm shows the
findings in the photograph. Which of the following ligand → receptor pairs most likely played a primary role in the
proliferation of the T lymphocytes present at the site of the rash in this patient?

question: During a study of muscle metabolism, hyperglycemia and hyperinsulinemia are induced in six healthy volunteers. Analysis of resting muscle biopsy samples reveals markedly elevated malonyl-CoA levels. Which metabolic process is most likely being directly inhibited by this rise in malonyl-CoA?
original_question:
Six healthy subjects participate in a study of muscle metabolism during which hyperglycemia and hyperinsulinemia
is induced. Muscle biopsy specimens obtained from the subjects during the resting state show significantly increased
concentrations of malonyl-CoA. The increased malonyl-CoA concentration most likely directly inhibits which of the
following processes in these subjects?

question: A new tyrosine kinase inhibitor is being investigated for its efficacy against chronic myeloid leukemia in blast crisis. In the investigation, all participants receive the drug at assigned doses ranging from 300 to 1000 mg/day, with cohorts of six to eight patients per dose level. The primary outcome is the assessment of antileukemic activity, measured by serial complete blood counts and bone marrow examinations over one year. Which of the following best characterizes this study design?
original_question:
Over 1 year, a study is conducted to assess the antileukemic activity of a new tyrosine kinase inhibitor in patients
with chronic myeloid leukemia in blast crisis. All patients enrolled in the study are informed that they would be
treated with the tyrosine kinase inhibitor. They are assigned to successive dose cohorts of 300 to 1000 mg/day of the
drug. Six to eight patients are assigned to each dose. Treatment efficacy is determined based on the results of
complete blood counts and bone marrow assessments conducted regularly throughout the study. This study is best
described as which of the following?

question: A 63-year-old man presents with a 4-day history of progressive left calf pain and swelling, along with a 1-month history of worsening upper midthoracic back pain and an unintentional 9-kg weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes only ibuprofen. Vital signs are normal. Physical exam reveals palpable lower extremity pulses and is otherwise unremarkable. Thoracic spine x-ray is normal. Abdominal CT demonstrates a 3-cm pancreatic body mass with liver metastases and superior mesenteric artery encasement. Left lower extremity ultrasonography reveals a femoropopliteal deep vein thrombosis. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of these findings?

options.D: Splenic artery aneurysm and embolic disease of the left lower extremity

original_question:
A 63-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 4-day history of increasingly severe left leg
pain and swelling of his left calf. He also has a 1-month history of increasingly severe upper midthoracic back pain.
During this time, he has had a 9- kg (20-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He has no history of major
medical illness. His only medication is ibuprofen. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is
25 kg/m2. His vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, lower extremity pulses are palpable bilaterally.
The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. An x-ray of the thoracic spine shows no
abnormalities. A CT scan of the abdomen shows a 3-cm mass in the body of the pancreas; there are liver metastases
and encasement of the superior mesenteric artery. Ultrasonography of the left lower extremity shows a
femoropopl iteal venous clot. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

question: A 40-year-old woman is evaluated for a 6-month history of hirsutism and secondary amenorrhea of 4 months' duration. Her vital signs are normal, and her BMI is 26 kg/m². Examination reveals temporal hair recession, coarse facial hair, and clitoromegaly. Laboratory studies show an elevated serum testosterone level, but normal serum androstenedione, dehydroepiandrosterone, and urinary 17-ketosteroid levels. Pelvic ultrasonography reveals a 12-cm ovarian mass. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this mass?
original_question:
A 40-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of increased facial hair growth. Her last
menstrual period was 4 months ago. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (154 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Her
pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Physical examination shows temporal balding and coarse dark
hair on the upper lip and chin. Pelvic examination shows clitoral enlargement. Her serum testosterone concentration
is increased. Serum concentrations of androstenedione, dehydroepiandrosterone, and urinary 17-ketosteroids are
within the reference ranges. Ultrasonography of the pelvis shows a 12-cm ovarian mass. Which of the following best
describes this mass?
(A)Granulosa tumor

question: A 35-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of pain and swelling in his right arm following a scrape from a tree branch. He has a temperature of 38.3°C (101°F). On examination, the right forearm shows a fluctuant, erythematous lesion at the injury site with significant tenderness to palpation and surrounding edema. What is the most likely primary pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for the edema?
original_question:
A 35-year-old man comes to the physician because of pain and swelling of his right arm where he scraped it on a
tree branch 2 days ago. His temperature is 38.3°C (101°F). Examination of the right forearm shows edema around a
fluctuant erythematous lesion at the site of trauma. The area is extremely tender to palpation. Which of the following
is most likely the primary mechanism of the development of edema in this patient?

question: A 12-year-old boy presents with a 2-month history of headaches and 6 days of nausea and vomiting. Physical examination reveals bilateral papilledema and a broad-based gait. An MRI demonstrates a pineal region tumor causing brain stem compression and hydrocephalus. Which of the following oculomotor functions is most likely impaired?
original_question:
A 12-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-month history of headaches and a 6-day history of
nausea and vomiting. Funduscopic examination shows bilateral papilledema. He walks with a broad-based gait. An
MRI of the brain shows a tumor in the pineal region compressing the brain stem and leading to hydrocephalus. This
patient most likely has impairment of which of the following oculomotor functions?

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 1-day history of nausea, vomiting, and right-sided abdominal pain that radiates to the back. He reports no fever or urinary frequency. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and atrial fibrillation. His medications are atorvastatin, glyburide, hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and warfarin. He has a 35-pack-year smoking history but quit 6 months ago, and he denies recent alcohol or illicit drug use. He is 188 cm tall and weighs 109 kg (BMI 31 kg/m²). Vital signs are temperature 36.4°C, pulse 88/min, respirations 20/min, and blood pressure 165/92 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation on room air is 96%. Examination reveals tenderness in the right upper quadrant and flank without rebound or guarding. An abdominal CT scan with and without contrast demonstrates an enlarged right kidney with wedge-shaped hypodense areas and no nephrolithiasis. What is the most significant predisposing risk factor for this patient's condition?
original_question:
A 52-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of nausea, vomiting, and right-
sided abdominal pain that radiates to his back. He has not had fever or increased urinary frequency. He has a history
of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and atrial fibrillation. Current medications are
atorvastatin, glyburide, hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and warfarin. He smoked 1½ packs of cigarettes daily for 35
years until he quit 6 months ago. He has had no recent alcoholic beverage or illicit drug use. He is 188 cm (6 ft 2 in)
tall and weighs 109 kg (240 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m2. Temperature is 36.4°C (97.6°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are
20/min, and blood pressure is 165/92 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of
96%. Physical examination discloses right upper quadrant and flank tenderness but no rebound tenderness or
guarding. CT scan of the abdomen with and without contrast shows an enlarged right kidney and wedge-shaped
areas of hypodensity; there is no evidence of nephrolithiasis. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing
risk factor for this patient's condition?

question: A 24-year-old male presents with a 2-day history of an erythematous, pruritic rash affecting his buttocks and lower extremities. He reports returning from a Caribbean cruise 4 days ago, where he swam in the ship’s pool and utilized the hot tub. The patient appears well, with normal vital signs. Physical examination reveals the findings shown in the provided image. In which of the following sites did the infectious agent responsible for these manifestations most likely initiate proliferation?
original_question:
A 24-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-day history of a red, itchy rash on his buttocks and legs. Four
days ago, he returned from a cruise to the Caribbean, during which he swam in the ship’s pool and used the hot tub.
He appears well. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows the findings in the
photograph. The infectious agent causing these findings most likely began to proliferate in which of the following
locations?

question: A 45-year-old man with no significant past medical history presents with a 3-week history of worsening epigastric burning discomfort and unintentional weight loss of 4.5 kg (10 lb). The pain is worse at night and typically begins 1 to 3 hours after eating. He reports similar but milder symptoms over the past year. On examination, there is deep epigastric tenderness. Stool is positive for occult blood. Upper endoscopy reveals a 3-cm actively bleeding gastric antral ulcer. A photomicrograph of Steiner silver-stained gastric mucosa biopsy (400x magnification) taken near the ulcer is provided. Which of the following mechanisms is most likely responsible for this condition?

options.A: Elaboration of proteases and urease with local tissue destruction
options.C: Ingestion of preformed toxins in contaminated well water

answer: Elaboration of proteases and urease with local tissue destruction
original_question:
An otherwise healthy 45-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of progressive epigastric
heartburn and a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss. The pain tends to be more severe at night and occurs 1 to 3 hours after
meals during the day. He has had similar episodes with lesser intensity during the past year. Abdominal examination
shows tenderness to deep palpation. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Endoscopy shows a bleeding 3-cm
ulcer in the antrum of the stomach. A photomicrograph of Steiner silver-stained tissue (400x) from a biopsy of the
gastric mucosa adjacent to the ulcer is shown. Which of the following processes is most likely to be involved?

question: A 14-year-old male presents to the emergency department following blunt trauma from a baseball bat to the lateral aspect of his leg just inferior to the knee. Physical examination reveals an inability to dorsiflex the foot. Which nerve is most probably affected?
original_question:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after being hit with a baseball bat on the lateral side of
his leg immediately below the knee. He is unable to dorsiflex his foot. Which of the following nerves is most likely
injured?

question: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe back and abdominal pain for 8 hours, accompanied by mild persistent vaginal bleeding. Abdominal ultrasonography reveals a ruptured 2-cm ectopic pregnancy in the ampullary portion of the fallopian tube. Into which anatomical space will the fluid from this rupture most likely accumulate?
original_question:
A 26-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of an 8-hour history of severe back and
abdominal pain and mild but persistent vaginal bleeding. Ultrasonography of the abdomen shows a 2-cm ectopic
pregnancy in the ampulla. The ampulla has ruptured into the surrounding tissue. Fluid from this rupture will most
likely be found in which of the following locations?

question: A 46-year-old female with active ankylosing spondylitis continues to have symptoms despite trials of multiple NSAIDs and sulfasalazine. Which cytokine's inhibition would be the most suitable next therapeutic target?
original_question:
A 46-year-old woman with active ankylosing spondylitis comes to the office for a follow-up examination. The use 
of various conventional nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs has been ineffective. Sulfasalazine treatment also has 
not resulted in improvement. The most appropriate next step in treatment is administration of a drug that inhibits  
which of the following?

question: A 55-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of recurrent, widespread blisters. On examination, lesions are most abundant in flexural regions such as the axillae and groin. The blisters are not easily ruptured, and no oral lesions are noted. Which adhesion failure is most likely responsible for these findings?
original_question:
A 55-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of recurrent, widespread blister formation.  
Physical examination shows lesions that are most numerous in the flexural areas including the axillae and groin. The 
blisters do not break easily, and there are no oral lesions. These blisters are most likely the result of adhesion failure  
involving which of the following?

question: A 17-year-old female presents to the emergency department after being discovered unresponsive near an empty acetaminophen bottle. The ingestion is estimated to have occurred 12 hours prior to arrival. Laboratory studies reveal a significantly elevated serum acetaminophen level. Following administration of activated charcoal and acetylcysteine, the patient develops jaundice and has an AST of 3000 U/L approximately two days later. What is the most likely cause of the jaundice?
original_question:
A 17-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after her boyfriend found her unconscious next 
to an empty bottle of acetaminophen. It is suspected that she ingested the tablets approximately 12 hours ago. Her 
serum acetaminophen concentration is markedly increased. Treatment with activated charcoal and acetylcysteine is 
initiated. Approximately 48 hours later, the patient develops jaundice, and her serum AST activity is 3000 U/L. 
Which of the following best explains the jaundice in this patient?

question: A 72-year-old frail woman presents with a 3-day history of fever, dyspnea, dysphagia, chest pain, and cough. On examination, she is tachypneic with bilaterally equal pulses. Chest percussion reveals dullness over the right lower lung field. Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates hypoxemia and a low PCO2. A chest radiograph shows opacification in the right lower lung zone. What is the most likely etiology?
original_question:
A 72-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of fever, shortness of breath, difficulty  
swallowing, chest pain, and cough. She is frail. Physical examination shows tachypnea and equal pulses bilaterally.  
Percussion of the chest shows dullness over the right lower lung field. Laboratory studies show arterial hypoxemia  
and decreased PCO 2. A chest x-ray shows an area of opacification in the lower region of the right lung. Which of the 
following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?

question: A 62-year-old man is hospitalized for assessment of abdominal pain. Throughout the interview, his replies are limited to single words and occasionally sarcastic remarks. He avoids eye contact and frowns while stating that he has already been asked these questions multiple times. What is the most appropriate next step for the physician?

options.A: "Can you tell me more about your abdominal pain?"
options.B: "I understand that you do not feel well, but I need to get a history here."
options.C: "I'll come back once you've calmed down, and we can talk more then."
options.D: "Listen, if you choose not to cooperate, I'm not going to be able to help you."
options.E: "You sound upset. Tell me a little more about that."

answer: "You sound upset. Tell me a little more about that."
original_question:
A 62-year-old man is admitted to the hospital for evaluation of abdominal pain. During the interview, he responds to
the questions with a single word and sometimes with sarcastic answers. He does not engage in eye contact, and he
frowns as he tells the physician that this is the third time he has been asked these questions. Which of the following
is the most appropriate response by the physician?

question: A 54-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of syncopal episodes and fatigue that interferes with his daily activities. His medical history is significant for a myocardial infarction 12 years prior. Vital signs reveal a heart rate of 40/min, respiratory rate of 18/min, and blood pressure of 138/85 mm Hg. On physical exam, cannon a waves are noted. An ECG demonstrates a P-wave rate of 90/min and an R-wave rate of 40/min, with dissociation between the atrial and ventricular activity. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 54-year-old man comes to the physician because of episodes of fainting for 3 months. He also has had difficulty
performing daily tasks because he is easily fatigued. He had a myocardial infarction 12 years ago. His pulse is
40/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 138/85 mm Hg. Physical examination shows evidence of
cannon a waves. An ECG shows a P-wave rate of 90/min, and an R-wave rate of 40/min, with no apparent relation
between the two. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question:
A 42-year-old woman presents for a routine health assessment. She reports feeling generally well, with intermittent constipation, abdominal discomfort, and mild fatigue. Her past medical history includes treatment for a renal calculus 10 years ago and a diagnosis of a "lazy gallbladder." Vital signs show a pulse of 82/min and blood pressure of 150/80 mm Hg. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory results are as follows:
- Erythrocyte count: 3 million/mm³
- Serum K⁺: 4.5 mEq/L
- Serum Cl⁻: 107 mEq/L
- Serum Ca²⁺: 12 mg/dL
- Serum phosphorus: 2.2 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 95 U/L
Which of the following locations is the most likely site of a small, well-defined nodule responsible for this patient's condition?
original_question:
A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine examination. She says that she has felt well except for
occasional episodes of constipation, abdominal discomfort, and mild fatigue. She was treated for a renal calculus 10
years ago and was told she had a "lazy gallbladder." Her pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg.
Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:
Erythrocyte count  3 million/mm3 
Serum  
K+ 4.5 mEq/L  
Cl– 107 mEq/L  
Ca2+ 12 mg/dL  
Phosphorus  2.2 mg/dL  
Alkaline phosphatase  95 U/L  
The most likely cause of this patient's condition is a small, well-defined nodule in which of the following locations?

question: A 68-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis for one year presents for a follow-up visit. She has had multiple recent hospitalizations for fluid overload secondary to nonadherence with her dietary regimen. She expresses frustration, stating, "I am tired of being on dialysis. Sticking to the restrictions is very difficult." What is the most suitable first statement for the physician to make?

options.A: "I imagine being on dialysis is frustrating, but you don't want to die from kidney disease, do you?"
options.B: "I know that this is challenging, but do you remember how difficult it is to be hospitalized?"
options.C: "It is tough to change your diet and fluid intake, but what sorts of things were you doing at first when you were following the recommendations?"
options.D: "Making these changes is so difficult. Can you imagine how good you will feel once you make the changes again?"
options.E: "This is very hard to do, but you may have an easier time if you met with the dietician again."

answer: "It is tough to change your diet and fluid intake, but what sorts of things were you doing at first when you were following the recommendations?"
original_question:
A 68-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease comes to the office for a follow-up examination. She has
required hemodialysis for the past year. Initially, she did well, but within the past 3 months, she has been admitted to
the hospital for fluid overload because of poor adherence to fluid and salt restrictions. She says, "I'm so sick of being
on dialysis. Following the restrictions is really hard." Which of the following is the most appropriate initial response
by the physician?

question: A researcher performs Southern blot analysis on DNA from various tissues. The DNA is first digested with a restriction enzyme, then separated by gel electrophoresis, and finally transferred to a membrane. The membrane is hybridized with a cDNA probe specific for a single immunoglobulin constant region. A diagram illustrates the resulting band patterns. Assuming the procedure was technically sound, these results are most consistent with which biological process?
original_question:
During an experiment, a Southern blot analysis is done by digesting DNA samples with a single restriction
endonuclease, separating the digestion products by gel electrophoresis, and transferring them to a filter. The
investigator probes the filter by exposing it to a cDNA clone that encodes a single immunoglobulin-constant region.
The figure shows the resulting pattern with DNA samples isolated from different organs. Assuming there were no
technical errors, the Southern blot analysis results demonstrate which of the following processes?

question: A 17-year-old adolescent with primary amenorrhea is noted on physical exam to have a typical female body habitus, normal breast development, and unremarkable external genitalia. She is found to have absence of axillary and pubic hair and declines a pelvic or rectal examination. What is the most probable underlying cause for these findings?
original_question:
A 17-year-old girl has never had a menstrual period. Physical examination shows a normal female body habitus,
normal breast development, and normal appearing external genitalia. She has no axillary or pubic hair. The patient
refuses to have a pelvic or rectal examination. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the clinical
presentation?

question: A 16-year-old male presents with a 3-day history of abdominal discomfort, emesis, and reduced food intake. The pain, initially localized to the right side, is now diffuse. He is febrile at 38.8°C (101.8°F), with a pulse of 100/min, respiratory rate of 20/min, and blood pressure of 143/83 mm Hg. Abdominal exam reveals guarding and diffuse rebound tenderness without palpable masses. CT imaging demonstrates a perforated appendix. Which organism is most likely identified in analysis of this patient's peritoneal fluid?
original_question:
A 16-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-day history of abdominal pain and vomiting; he also
has had decreased appetite during this period. The pain was initially on the right but now has become generalized.
His temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is
143/83 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows guarding with diffuse rebound tenderness. There are no palpable
masses. A CT scan of the abdomen shows a perforated appendix. Examination of peritoneal fluid from this patient
will most likely show which of the following organisms?

question: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of amenorrhea accompanied by hot flashes, night sweats, and sleep disturbances. She believes she is in menopause and inquires about pharmacologic management for her symptoms. Physical examination is unremarkable, and a urine pregnancy test is negative. After the physician explains that hormone therapy is a potential option and reviews the associated risks, which of the following is the most suitable next statement from the physician?

options.A: "Could you tell me your thoughts about the hormone treatment option we have discussed?"
options.B: "I have many patients with similar symptoms, and everyone responds to hormone therapy differently."
options.C: "I will give you some written information to review, and then I can answer any remaining questions."
options.D: "If your symptoms are really severe, the risk of hormone therapy may be worth it.”
options.E: "Would you like me to tell you what I think you should do?"

answer: "Could you tell me your thoughts about the hormone treatment option we have discussed?"
original_question:
A 45-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of hot flashes, night sweats, and insomnia.
She has not had a menstrual period during this time. She thinks she is going through menopause and asks the
physician if there are any medications that will alleviate her symptoms. Physical examination shows no
abnormalities. The result of a urine pregnancy test is negative. The physician explains that hormone therapy likely
will help and explains the risks to the patient. Which of the following is the next most appropriate physician
statement?

question: A randomized trial evaluates the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse effects in children with pertussis treated with azithromycin versus erythromycin. Among 100 enrolled children, 50 are assigned to each treatment. Vomiting occurs in 5 patients receiving azithromycin and in 15 patients receiving erythromycin. Which of the following most accurately indicates the absolute risk reduction for vomiting in the azithromycin group?
original_question:
A randomized controlled trial is conducted to assess the risk for development of gastrointestinal adverse effects
using azithromycin compared with erythromycin in the treatment of pertussis in children. Of the 100 children with
pertussis enrolled, 50 receive azithromycin, and 50 receive erythromycin. Results show vomiting among 5 patients
in the azithromycin group, compared with 15 patients in the erythromycin group. Which of the following best
represents the absolute risk reduction for vomiting among patients in the azithromycin group?

question:
A 34-year-old female with a decade-long history of hepatitis C infection presents with a one-month history of worsening fatigue. Vital signs are normal. On examination, the liver is not palpable. Laboratory results are as follows:
- Albumin: 3 g/dL
- Total bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 115 U/L
- AST: 53 U/L
- ALT: 48 U/L
Liver biopsy reveals focal hepatocellular injury with lymphocytic infiltration and early fibrosis. What is the most probable mechanism for the persistent hepatocyte damage in this individual?

options.A: Foreign peptides bound to class I MHC molecules are recognized by CD4+ T lymphocytes
options.B: Foreign peptides bound to class I MHC molecules are recognized by CD8+ T lymphocytes
options.C: Foreign peptides bound to class II MHC molecules are recognized by CD8+ T lymphocytes
options.D: Self-peptides bound to class I MHC molecules are recognized by CD8+ T lymphocytes
options.E: Self-peptides bound to class II MHC molecules are recognized by CD4+ T lymphocytes

answer: Foreign peptides bound to class I MHC molecules are recognized by CD8+ T lymphocytes
original_question:
A 34-year-old woman with a 10-year history of hepatitis C comes to the physician because of progressive fatigue
during the past month. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows that the liver is not
palpable. Serum studies show:
Albumin  3 g/dL  
Total bilirubin  1.8 mg/dL  
Alkaline phosphatase  115 U/L  
AST  53 U/L  
ALT  48 U/L  
A liver biopsy specimen shows focal areas of hepatocellular injury with infiltration of lymphocytes and early 
fibrosis. Which of the following mechanisms is the most likely cause of the ongoing hepatocyte injury in this 
patient?

question: A 50-year-old woman presents for a follow-up visit. She has had blood pressure readings of 145/100 mm Hg and 145/95 mm Hg at prior appointments. Current vital signs include a pulse of 75/min, respirations of 15/min, and a blood pressure of 150/95 mm Hg. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. If no treatment is initiated, which parameter is most likely to decline in this patient?
original_question:
A 50-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Her blood pressure was 145/100 mm Hg
and 145/95 mm Hg, respectively, at two previous visits. Today, her pulse is 75/min, respirations are 15/min, and
blood pressure is 150/95 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. If left untreated, which of the
following is most likely to decrease in this patient?

question:
A 62-year-old man with a known diagnosis of chronic inflammatory interstitial pneumonitis undergoes a follow-up evaluation. Pulmonary function tests are performed, followed by a biopsy of the involved lung tissue. Which of the following patterns of alveolar cell population changes is most likely to be seen on analysis of this biopsy specimen compared to that of a healthy individual?
Type I Pneumocytes  Type II Pneumocytes  Fibroblasts
original_question:
A 62-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination after he was diagnosed with chronic
inflammatory interstitial pneumonitis. Following pulmonary function testing, a biopsy specimen of the affected area
of the lungs is obtained. Compared with a healthy man, analysis of this patient's biopsy specimen is most likely to
show which of the following patterns of changes in the cell populations of alveoli?
Type I Pneumocytes  Type II Pneumocytes  Fibroblasts

question: A 31-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus presents for evaluation of a foul-smelling, draining foot wound that developed over the past 48 hours. On examination, there is a 4-cm area of necrosis with a purplish-black discoloration on the heel. Crepitant bullae are present, releasing abundant serous fluid. A Gram stain of a tissue biopsy reveals gram-positive rods. Which virulence factor is most likely produced by the causative organism?
original_question:
A 31-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the physician because of an oozing, foul-smelling
wound on her foot for 2 days. Physical examination shows a 4-cm, necrotizing wound with a purplish black
discoloration over the heel. Crepitant bullae producing profuse amounts of serous drainage are seen. A Gram stain of
a tissue biopsy specimen shows gram-positive rods. The causal organism most likely produces which of the
following virulence factors?

question: A 4-month-old infant with severe combined immunodeficiency develops a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on his trunk 6 days after bone marrow transplantation. A skin biopsy reveals diffuse vacuolar degeneration of the epidermal basal layer accompanied by a mononuclear cell infiltrate. Which of the following is the most likely etiology for this rash?
original_question:
A 4-month-old boy with severe combined immunodeficiency receives a bone marrow transplant. Six days later, he
develops a widespread, erythematous, maculopapular rash over the trunk. Examination of a skin biopsy specimen
shows diffuse vacuolar degeneration of basal epidermal cells with a mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrate. Which
of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s rash?

question: A 37-year-old female presents with acute dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain, along with preexisting edema of the right lower extremity. She is diagnosed with pulmonary embolism. Which physical examination finding, if identified, would most specifically suggest pulmonary arterial hypertension in this individual?
original_question:
A 37-year-old woman with right lower extremity edema is evaluated because of the sudden onset of shortness of
breath and pleuritic chest pain. A diagnosis of pulmonary embolism is made. Which of the following signs, if
present on physical examination, would be the most specific indicator of pulmonary arterial hypertension in this
patient?

question: A 43-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus presents to the emergency department with nausea and vomiting for 12 hours, which started after consuming champagne the prior evening. She is currently on a 5-day regimen of metronidazole for a recent diagnosis of *Trichomonas vaginalis* infection. Her other medications are acetaminophen, lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and metformin. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.2°C (99°F), heart rate of 110/min, and blood pressure of 125/82 mm Hg. On exam, she appears pale with dry mucous membranes. What is the most probable etiology for her nausea and vomiting?
original_question:
A 43-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department because of a 12-hour
history of nausea and vomiting. She says that it began after drinking champagne at a birthday party the previous
night. Three days ago, she was diagnosed with Trichomonas vaginalis  infection, and a 5-day course of
metronidazole was initiated. Current medications also include acetaminophen, lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and
metformin. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 125/82 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows pallor and dry mucous membranes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this
patient's nausea and vomiting?

question: A 45-year-old man presents with right shoulder pain that started following a wood-chopping activity two days prior. On examination of the right upper extremity, there are no apparent bony deformities or localized tenderness. Pain is elicited when the patient externally rotates the shoulder against resistance, without associated weakness. Besides the teres minor tendon, which other tendon is most likely inflamed in this individual?
original_question:
A 45-year-old man comes to the physician because of right shoulder pain that began after he chopped wood 2 days
ago. Examination of the right upper extremity shows no obvious bone deformities or point tenderness. The pain is
reproduced when the patient is asked to externally rotate the shoulder against resistance; there is no weakness. In
addition to the teres minor, inflammation of which of the following tendons is most likely in this patient?

question: A 44-year-old man presents with a 6-week progression of numbness in his feet. He finished a chemotherapy regimen containing vincristine for small cell lung cancer two months prior. On examination, there is distal foot muscle weakness, absent ankle reflexes, and diminished sensation in the distal lower extremities. Which of the following mechanisms is most likely responsible for these findings?

options.A: Apoptosis of dorsal root ganglion neurons
options.C: Decreased expression of myelin protein in Schwann cells

original_question:
A 44-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-week history of progressive numbness of his feet.
Two months ago, he completed a course of chemotherapy, including vincristine, for small cell lung carcinoma.
Physical examination shows muscle weakness of the distal portion of the feet and absent ankle jerk reflexes.
Sensation is decreased over the distal portions of the lower extremities. This patient's findings are most likely caused
by which of the following mechanisms?

question: A 10-year-old girl is evaluated due to a school nurse's concern for Marfan syndrome. Her height is at the 95th percentile and her weight at the 25th percentile. On examination, she has a high-arched palate with dental crowding, long and slender limbs, and hypermobile joints. Genetic analysis of the FBN1 gene identifies a single nucleotide variant that does not result in an amino acid substitution. Her mother, who is 163 cm tall and shows no signs of Marfan syndrome, is found to have the same genetic variant. This specific nucleotide change is also present in 15 out of 200 individuals in the general population who do not have the syndrome. Which of the following is the most accurate classification for this genetic variant found in the patient and her mother?

options.A: It is a disease-causing mutation in the patient and her mother
options.C: It is a sequence variant of unknown significance
options.D: It will change the folding of the protein
options.E: It will result in a truncated protein

original_question:
A 10-year-old girl is brought to the office by her mother because her school nurse thinks that she may have Marfan
syndrome. She is at the 95th percentile for height and 25th percentile for weight. Physical examination shows a
narrow palate with dental crowding, long slender extremities, and joint laxity. Molecular testing for FBN1  shows a
single nucleotide difference that does not change the amino acid at that locus. Her mother is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall.
There is no clinical evidence of Marfan syndrome in the mother, but she has the same single nucleotide change as
the patient. The same nucleotide change is found in 15 of 200 individuals without Marfan syndrome. Which of the
following best describes the single nucleotide change in the patient and her mothe r?

question: A 26-year-old woman presents to a crowded emergency department with a two-day history of rhinorrhea. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Her vital signs are normal. After waiting six hours to be seen, she expresses anger toward the staff, stating her lack of insurance is the sole reason for the delay. After apologizing, which of the following opening statements by the physician would be most appropriate?

options.A: "It is harsh to suggest that you waited because you don’t have insurance, but how can we help you feel better today?"
options.B: "It sounds like you are angry; shall I come back in a few minutes?"
options.C: "Our wait time has nothing to do with insurance, so let’s talk abo ut the reason you are here."
options.D: "The reason you waited so long is because we need to triage by severity."
options.E: "Thank you for waiting. How can I help you today?"

answer: "Thank you for waiting. How can I help you today?"
original_question:
A 26-year-old woman comes to a busy emergency department because of a 2-day history of runny nose. She has no
history of major medical illness and takes no medications. Her vital signs are within normal limits. The patient has
to wait 6 hours before she is seen by a physician. She is angry with the staff and says the only reason she had to wait
this long is because she does not have insurance. In addition to apologizing to the patient, which of the following is
the most appropriate opening remark by the physician?

question:
A 26-year-old man arrives in the emergency department via ambulance 30 minutes following a gunshot wound to his leg. He is unresponsive and has notable pallor. Vital signs show a pulse of 120/min, respirations of 16/min, and a blood pressure of 80/60 mm Hg. Compared to a healthy adult, which of the following parameters is most likely to be observed in this patient?
Arterial Baroreceptor  Systemic Vascular  Pulmonary Vascular  Systemic Capillary  
 Firing Rate        Resistance        Resistance        Fluid Transfer
original_question:
A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 30 minutes after being shot in the leg. He
is unconscious and appears markedly pale. His pulse is 120/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is
80/60 mm Hg. Compared with a healthy adult, which of the following findings is most likely in this patient?
Arterial Baroreceptor  Systemic Vascular Pulmonary Vascular  Systemic Capillary  
 Firing Rate  Resistance   Resistance   Fluid Transfer

question: A 36-year-old man with severe intellectual disability, who is nonverbal, presents with a two-week history of progressive abdominal distention. He is accompanied by caregivers from his residential facility, and no prior medical records are obtainable. The physical exam reveals a distended abdomen with shifting dullness and a positive fluid wave; there is no evidence of abdominal trauma. Laboratory test results are within normal limits. An abdominal CT scan is obtained. The arrow on the scan points to fluid located in which anatomic space?
original_question:
A 36-year-old man with profound intellectual disability is brought to the physician by staff at his facility because of
increasing abdominal girth during the past 2 weeks. He is unable to speak, and no medical history is currently
available. Physical examination shows a protuberant abdomen with a fluid wave and shifting dullness. There are no
signs of trauma to the area. Laboratory studies show no abnormalities. A CT scan of the abdomen is shown. Fluid is
present in which of the following areas as indicated by the arrow?

question:
A novel serum assay is designed to identify a tumor-specific protein. Based on the initial validation data shown below, what is the probability that a patient from this study population with a positive test result actually has the tumor?

Tumor  
Present  Absent  
Test Result  Positive  40 20 60 
Negative  10 30 40 
50 50 100
original_question:
A new test has been developed to detect the presence of a tumor-specific protein in serum. The initial evaluation of
this test shows:
Tumor  
Present  Absent  
Test Result  Positive  40 20 60 
Negative  10 30 40 
50 50 100 
Which of the following is the likelihood that a patient with a positive test from this sample has a tumor?

question: A 75-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and hypertension is evaluated for a 4-month history of anorexia and a corresponding 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss. She moved to the United States from Argentina 35 years ago. Her primary language is Spanish, and she speaks very little English. Her daughter, who is present, is bilingual. The physician has a basic knowledge of Spanish from high school, and the clinic receptionist is also bilingual. While a certified interpreter is not on-site, a telephone interpretation service can be used. Who is the most suitable interpreter for this patient visit?
original_question:
A 75-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension is brought to the office by her daughter  
because of a 4-month history of loss of appetite. The patient has had a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss during this time.  
She immigrated to the USA from Argentina 35 years ago. She is fluent in Spanish and has limited English language  
proficiency. The patient’s daughter is bilingual. The physician studied Spanish in high school. The clinic  
receptionist is bilingual. A certified interpreter is not available at the clinic, but a telephone interpreter service is 
available. Which of the following is the most appropriate person to serve as an interpreter for this patient encounter?

question: In a vascular bath preparation of a healthy animal kidney, the afferent arteriole is maintained at a constant pressure of 100 mm Hg. If the efferent arteriole is then mechanically constricted, which Starling force across the glomerular capillary wall is most directly and primarily altered?
original_question:
During a study of renal glomeruli, a healthy animal kidney is kept in a vascular bath preparation at a constant 
afferent arterial pressure of 100 mm Hg. If the efferent arteriole is constricted with a vascular clamp, which of the 
following Starling forces is most likely to change in the glomeruli?

question: During a routine health assessment, a 2-year-old boy is noted to be irritable, avoids eye contact, and displays hand-flapping. He has a history of normal prenatal and birth parameters. Growth measurements show his head circumference is at the 90th percentile, while his height and weight are at lower percentiles. His language development was delayed at 15 months. There is a family history of learning difficulties and cognitive disabilities in his parents and a maternal uncle. What is the most probable etiology for this presentation?

options.A: Creation of an alternative splice site

original_question:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. He was delivered at term after an 
uncomplicated pregnancy. His birth weight was 3500 g (7 lb 11 oz), and Apgar scores were 8 and 10 at 
1 and 5 minutes, respectively. At the age of 15 months, physical examination showed no abnormalities, but he was 
not yet talking. Both of his parents had learning difficulties in school, and his mother stopped attending after the 
10th grade. He has a maternal uncle with cognitive disabilities. He is at the 25th percentile for height,  
15th percentile for weight, and 90th percentile for head circumference. He appears irritable, he resists making eye 
contact, and he is flapping his hands. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?

question: A 55-year-old woman with a parathyroid adenoma, identified by neck ultrasonography as a hypoechoic mass posterior to the left thyroid lobe, is scheduled for surgical excision. To achieve hemostasis during resection, which vessel should be ligated?
original_question:
A 55-year-old woman is diagnosed with parathyroid adenoma after ultrasonography of the neck showed 
a soft-tissue, hypoechoic mass at the back of the left lobe of the thyroid bed. Surgical removal of the 
mass is planned, and the patient is admitted to the hospital. During this procedure, ligation of which of 
the following vessels is most appropriate to ensure hemostasis in this patient?

question: A 46-year-old woman presents with fatigue and generalized muscle weakness for two months. She has a ten-year history of hypertension managed with a thiazide diuretic and reports consuming five 12-ounce beers daily, with increased intake on weekends. Vitals show a seated pulse of 90/min and BP 105/60 mm Hg, while standing pulse is 95/min and BP 99/59 mm Hg. Reflexes are brisk. Lab results include hemoglobin 12 g/dL, hematocrit 35%, serum potassium 3.3 mEq/L, and glucose 110 mg/dL. What is the most probable underlying cause of these findings?
original_question:
A 46-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of fatigue and muscle weakness. She has
a 10-year history of hypertension treated with a thiazide diuretic. She drinks five 12-oz beers each evening and
sometimes more on weekends. Her pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg while seated; pulse is
95/min, and blood pressure is 99/59 mm Hg while standing. Physical examination shows brisk reflexes. Laboratory
studies show:
Hemoglobin  12 g/dL  
Hematocrit  35% 
Serum  
K+ 3.3 mEq/L  
Glucose  110 mg/dL  
Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?

question: A 55-year-old male executive is hospitalized for assessment of abdominal pain. While courteous to his physician, he is verbally abusive toward nursing and ancillary staff. During a visit from his wife and two of his three adult children, the patient expresses contempt, stating the absent child has always been without value. What is the most probable underlying cause for this patient's conduct?
original_question:
A 55-year-old man who is a business executive is admitted to the hospital for evaluation of abdominal pain. He is
polite to the physician but berates the nurses and other staff. The patient's wife and two of his three adult children
arrive for a visit. The patient says with disgust that the missing child is and always has been worthless. Which of the
following is the most likely explanation for this patient's behavior?

question: A clinical trial is conducted to assess the acceptability of acupuncture for pediatric patients experiencing chronic headaches. Sixty children with this condition are enrolled. All participants continue their standard treatment and additionally receive acupuncture sessions three times weekly over a two-month period. Which of the following most accurately characterizes this study's design?
original_question:
A study is designed to evaluate the feasibility of acupuncture in children with chronic headaches. Sixty children with
chronic headaches are recruited for the study. In addition to their usual therapy, all children are treated with
acupuncture three times a week for 2 months. Which of the following best describes this study design?

question: A 6-year-old girl presents with a 1-week history of progressive, constant neck pain and a 2-month history of severe headaches occurring three to four times per week, each lasting approximately 1 hour. Her past medical history includes four episodes of otitis media and three urinary tract infections over the last 4 years. On examination, her temperature is 37.6°C (99.6°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 95/63 mm Hg. Neck examination reveals no palpable masses, but generalized hyperreflexia and a positive Babinski sign are noted. CT imaging demonstrates bone destruction in the right mastoid and internal ear. A biopsy of the retropharyngeal area reveals aggregates of segmented neutrophils and evidence of Candida albicans. Immunologic studies indicate a neutrophil count of 9800/µL, with delayed bactericidal activity against Staphylococcus aureus. Which gene is most likely affected by a homozygous mutation in this patient?
original_question:
A 6-year-old girl is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-week history of constant increasingly severe neck pain
and a 2-month history of severe headaches that occur three to four times weekly and last for 1 hour. She also has had
four episodes of otitis media and three urinary tract infections during the past 4 years. Today, her temperature is
37.6°C (99.6°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 95/63 mm Hg. Examination of the
neck shows no palpable masses, but there is generalized hyperreflexia and Babinski sign is present. CT scan of the
mastoid area shows bone destruction in the right mastoid and right internal ear. Examination of a biopsy specimen of
the retropharyngeal area shows aggregates of segmented neutrophils as well as evidence of Candida albicans .
Immunologic studies show a neutrophil count of 9800/µL, but these cells show a delay in bactericidal activity
against Staphylococcus aureus . The most likely cause of this patient’s condition is a homozy gous mutation in which
of the following genes?

question: A 2-year-old boy is evaluated for a one-day history of his left thumb being acutely painful, swollen, and erythematous. His mother reports no known injury. He has had a decreased appetite but is otherwise active. Past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. On examination, he is crying. Vital signs include a temperature of 37.7°C (99.8°F). Notable physical findings are a vesicle in the mouth, enlarged cervical lymph nodes, and the digital appearance shown. What is the most likely category of pathogen responsible for the finger lesion?
original_question:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 1-day history of severe pain, swelling, and
redness of his left thumb. The mother does not recall any trauma to the area. She says he has been eating poorly
during this period, but otherwise he has been behaving normally. He has no history of major medical illness and
receives no medications. He appears tearful. He is at the 90th percentile for length and 80th percentile for weight.
His temperature is 37.7°C (99.8°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/50 mm Hg.
Physical examination shows an oral vesicle, cervical lymphadenopathy, and the findings in the photograph. Which
of the following types of infectious agents is the most likely cause of the findings in this patient’s finger?

question: A 7-year-old boy from Kentucky presents with a 2-week history of abdominal cramping and diarrhea. Three hours prior, he experienced fecal incontinence, and his mother observed a motile, worm-like organism in his underwear. The patient is afebrile and has no respiratory symptoms or bloody sputum. There is no recent travel history. Physical and rectal examinations are unremarkable. Through which of the following routes was the causative pathogen most likely transmitted?
original_question:
A 7-year-old boy who lives in Kentucky is brought to the office by his mother because of a 2-week history of
cramping abdominal pain and diarrhea. Three hours ago, the patient thought he had accidentally defecated in his
pants. The mother says that she looked in his underpants and saw something move, which she captured. She says
that it looked like an earthworm. The patient has had no fever, cough, or blood-tinged sputum. He has not traveled
recently. Physical examination, including rectal examination, shows no abnormalities. This patient most likely
acquired the causal infectious agent via which of the following modes of transmission?

question: A 23-year-old male with a history of multiple sexual partners presents with dysuria and a yellow urethral discharge. Gram stain of the exudate demonstrates abundant neutrophils containing intracellular gram-negative diplococci. He reports three prior episodes of urethritis over the last two years. Which characteristic of the causative organism is most responsible for this pattern of recurrent infection?
original_question:
A sexually active 23-year-old man with multiple sex partners has dysuria and a yellow urethral exudate. Gram stain
of the exudate shows numerous neutrophils, many that contain intracellular gram-negative diplococci. He has had
three similar episodes of urethritis over the past 2 years. Which of the following properties of the infecting organism
best explains the reinfection?

question: A 23-year-old woman presents for preconception genetic counseling. Her brother and maternal uncle both died from Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) at ages 28 and 17, respectively; neither relative underwent genetic testing before death. Her serum muscle creatine kinase level is 120 U/L (reference range 22–198 U/L). Her 50-year-old mother has a serum muscle creatine kinase level of 300 U/L. What is the most accurate determination of this patient's carrier status for DMD?

options.A: The patient has a 50% risk for developing DMD
options.B: The patient has a 50% risk of having a child with DMD
options.C: The patient is a carrier of the disease based on her family history of DMD
options.D: The patient is not a carrier of the DMD based on her normal creatine kinase concentration
options.E: The patient's DMD carrier status is uncertain because of random X inactivation

answer: The patient's DMD carrier status is uncertain because of random X inactivation
original_question:
A 23-year-old woman comes to the physician for genetic counseling prior to conception. Her brother and maternal
uncle had Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) and died at the ages of 28 and 17 years, respectively. Genetic
analysis was not performed on either relative prior to death. Serum studies show a muscle creatine kinase
concentration of 120 U/L (N=22 –198). The patient's 50-year-old mother has a serum muscle creatine kinase
concentration of 300 U/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate assessment of this patient's carrier status
for this disease?

question: A 20-year-old woman is evaluated for a 5-year history of menorrhagia severe enough to require changing sanitary protection every 20 minutes during her periods, which occur regularly every 28 days. She reports prolonged bleeding from a minor finger laceration. There is no history of easy bruising or weight change. She uses an oral contraceptive but is not sexually active. Vital signs are normal except for a temperature of 37.5°C (99.5°F). Examination reveals mild conjunctival pallor and a normal pelvic exam. Lab results are significant for a microcytic hypochromic anemia. Coagulation studies, including PT, PTT, and platelet count and function, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely hematologic disorder responsible for her menorrhagia?
original_question:
A 20-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 5-year history of heavy bleeding with menses that often
requires her to change her sanitary pads three times hourly. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She recently
sustained a minor cut to her finger, and the bleeding took longer to stop than usual. She has not had easy bruising or
change in weight. She only takes an oral contraceptive, but she has not been sexually active for the past 6 months.
Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 120/66 mm Hg.
Physical examination shows mildly pale conjunctivae. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory
studies show:
Hemoglobin  10.5 g/dL  
Hematocrit  31.3%  
Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration  28% Hb/cell  
Mean corpuscular volume  70 μm3 
Leukocyte count  5500/mm3 
Platelet count  275,000/mm3 
Platelet aggregation studies  normal  
Prothrombin time  10.5 sec (INR=1.0)  
Partial thromboplastin time  28 sec  
A Pap smear shows no abnormalities. Which of the following hematologic disorders is the most likely cause of this 
patient's menorrhagia?

question: A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department following a motorcycle collision. He has severe pelvic pain, perineal bruising, and pain with pelvic motion. Blood is visible at the urethral meatus in the absence of other penile trauma. Imaging confirms a fracture of the superior pubic ramus. If a urethral disruption is present, which segment of the urethra is most likely to be injured?
original_question:
A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after being struck by a car while driving his
motorcycle. He reports severe pelvic pain. On examination, there is bruising of the perineum and pain is elicited
with motion of his pelvis. Blood is noted at the urethral meatus. There is no other penile trauma. A plain x-ray
shows a fracture of the superior pubic ramus and retrograde urethrography is done to evaluate for a urethral
disruption. Which of the following portions of the urethra would be at greatest risk for injury in this patient?

question: A 63-year-old man is discovered unresponsive by law enforcement and arrives at the emergency department within one hour. He is breathing at a rate of 30 breaths per minute. Auscultation reveals crackles in the left upper and entire right lung fields. Despite adequate resuscitative efforts, the patient expires. An autopsy is performed, and a photomicrograph of a right lung section is provided. Which mediator is most responsible for the location of the cell indicated by the arrow?
original_question:
A 63-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after police found him unresponsive. His
respirations are 30/min. Crackles are heard over the left upper and the entire right lung fields. Despite appropriate
lifesaving measures, he dies. A photomicrograph of a section of the right lung obtained at autopsy is shown. Which
of the following mediators is the most likely cause of the position of the cell indicated by the arrow?

question: A 25-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of worsening low back pain and heavy menstrual bleeding. Her vital signs are within normal limits, and physical examination is unremarkable. Endometrial biopsy reveals tubular endometrial glands with numerous mitotic figures in both glands and stroma. Which protein or enzyme is primarily responsible for regulating cellular progression into this stage of the menstrual cycle?
original_question:
A 25-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of increasingly severe low back pain and
heavy menses. Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 75/min, respirations are 13/min, and blood pressure is
115/79 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. An endometrial biopsy specimen shows regular
tubular endometrial glands with abundant mitotic figures in the endometrial glands and stroma. Which of the
following proteins or enzymes regulate the progression of cells into this phase of this patient's menstrual cycle?

question: A 29-year-old woman with trigeminal neuralgia begins treatment with carbamazepine. Due to her significant family history of osteoporosis, her physician recommends she increase her dietary vitamin D. This recommendation is most likely made because carbamazepine alters which pharmacokinetic process?
original_question:
A 29-year-old woman is prescribed carbamazepine for trigeminal neuralgia. She has a strong family history of
osteoporosis. As a result, the physician also advises her to increase her intake of vitamin D. The most likely reason
for this recommendation is that carbamazepine may affect which of the following pharmacokinetic processes?

question:
An 18-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-day history of fever, dizziness, weakness, nausea, vomiting, and a rash. Two days ago, she began her menstrual period and started using synthetic tampons and sanitary pads. Her medical history is significant for recurrent urinary tract infections managed with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. She also takes aspirin and an herbal supplement as needed for dysmenorrhea. On examination, her temperature is 39°C (102.2°F), pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 85/55 mm Hg. Findings include injected conjunctivae, a fine erythematous rash on the trunk, palms, and soles, and erythema of the oral and vaginal mucosae. Lab results are as follows:
Leukocytes: 15,000/mm³ with 75% segmented neutrophils, 5% bands, 1% eosinophils, 15% lymphocytes, and 4% monocytes; Platelets: 100,000/mm³; AST: 100 U/L; ALT: 80 U/L.
Which of the patient's recent practices is the most probable risk factor for her current illness?
original_question:
An 18-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of fever, dizziness,
weakness, rash, nausea, and vomiting. She has been using synthetic sanitary pads and tampons since her last
menstrual period began 2 days ago. She has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections treated with trimethoprim-
sulfamethoxazole. Current medications also include aspirin and an herbal supplement for menstrual cramps as
needed. Her temperature is 39°C (102.2°F), pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 85/55 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows injected conjunctivae and a fine, erythematous rash over the trunk, palms, and soles of the feet.
Oral and pelvic examinations show erythema of the mucous membranes. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte count  15,000/mm3 
Segmented neutrophils  75% 
Bands  5% 
Eosinophils  1% 
Lymphocytes  15% 
Monocytes  4% 
Platelet count  100,000/mm3 
Serum  
AST  100 U/L  
ALT  80 U/L  
The patient's use of which of the following most likely increased her risk for this condition?

question: A 53-year-old male presents for evaluation after experiencing intermittent blood in his stool for the past 6 months. He denies pain during bowel movements. On physical exam, a 1-cm mass is visible at the anal verge, situated below the dentate line. A biopsy is planned. If malignancy is confirmed, which lymph node group should be assessed for potential metastatic spread?
original_question:
A 53-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of intermittent blood in his stool. He has
had no pain with defecation. Physical examination shows a 1-cm, visible anal mass located below the dentate line. A
biopsy of the mass is scheduled. If the mass is found to be malignant, it is most appropriate to evaluate which of the
following lymph nodes for possible metastasis?

question: A 14-year-old adolescent male presents for a pre-participation sports physical. He expresses embarrassment and hesitance to disrobe due to breast enlargement that has developed over the past year. His height and weight are at the 50th percentile. Examination reveals bilateral, tender 1.5-cm fibroglandular discs directly under the nipple-areolar complexes, with normal genitalia and Tanner stage 3 pubic hair. Serum levels of gonadotropins, estrogens, and testosterone are all within normal limits. What is the most probable etiology for his breast development?
original_question:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a physical examination prior to participating in sports. He appears
reluctant to remove his shirt for the examination, and says that he is embarrassed because he has grown breasts
during the past year. He is at the 50th percentile for height and weight. Physical examination shows bilateral 1.5-cm
fibroglandular masses located beneath the nipple-areolar complex and normal penis and testes. Pubic hair
development is Tanner stage 3. Serum concentrations of gonadotropic hormones, estrogens, and testosterone are
within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's breast enlargement?

question: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department 30 minutes after a motor vehicle crash in which he was an unrestrained driver and his face hit the steering wheel. He reports moderate facial pain and diplopia on upward gaze. His medical history is unremarkable and he takes no regular medications. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination of the left eye reveals periorbital ecchymosis, infraorbital edema, and restricted vertical ocular motility. The right eye is normal, and visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes. An orbital CT scan has been obtained. Based on this presentation, what additional finding would most likely be detected on the left side during the remainder of the physical examination?
original_question:
A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 30 minutes after he was involved in a
motor vehicle collision in which his face struck the steering wheel. He was the unrestrained driver. On arrival, he
has moderate facial pain and double vision on upward gaze. He has no history of serious illness and takes no
medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the left eye shows periorbital ecchymoses and
infraorbital edema. Ocular movement is restricted vertically. Examination of the right eye shows no abnormalities.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. CT scan of the orbits is shown. The remainder of the examination is most likely to
disclose impairment of which of the following on this patient's left side?

question: A 23-year-old previously healthy woman presents to a medical station shortly after collapsing at the end of a marathon. She remains alert and oriented, reporting dizziness and lightheadedness but no headache or nausea. She is not on any regular medications. Initial vital signs reveal tachycardia and hypotension, with a pulse of 120/min and a blood pressure of 85/50 mm Hg; the remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. After being placed in a supine position with her legs elevated and receiving an infusion of isotonic saline, her pulse decreases to 70/min and her blood pressure normalizes to 110/70 mm Hg within 30 minutes. She reports feeling well and is able to eat. Which of the following is the most accurate characterization of her condition?
original_question:
A 23-year-old woman is brought to the medical tent 2 minutes after she collapsed at the finish line of a marathon.
She has not lost consciousness; she is alert and coherent and says she feels dizzy and light-headed. She does not
have headache or nausea. She has no history of major medical illness and takes no medications. Her pulse is
120/min, and blood pressure is 85/50 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows
no other abnormalities. She is placed on a cot, her feet are elevated, and an infusion of 0.9% saline is begun. Thirty
minutes later, her pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. She says she feels well and begins eating
orange slices. Which of the following best describes this patient’s condition?

question: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-hour history of periumbilical pain, nausea, and a single episode of vomiting.
