question: A 57-year-old man with a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril is evaluated in the emergency department after collapsing in his kitchen 30 minutes ago. His initial vital signs were a temperature of 37.2°C, pulse of 90/min, labored respirations of 10/min, and blood pressure of 180/100 mm Hg. Following intubation and mechanical ventilation, his vitals remain unchanged, with an oxygen saturation of 98% on an FIO2 of 0.4. He is unresponsive with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3. Neurological examination reveals absent oculocephalic and corneal reflexes, as well as no eye movement with ice-water caloric testing. A head CT demonstrates a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. The patient's wife confirms he wished to be an organ donor but has no formal advance directive. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Consult with the hospital ethics committee
options.B: Contact the organ bank for potential donation
options.C: Determine if there are any patients awaiting organ donation in the hospital
options.D: Explain that the patient is not a candidate for organ donation because he does not have an advance directive
options.E: Explain that the patient is not a candidate for organ donation because of his history of hypertension

answer: Contact the organ bank for potential donation
original_question:
A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 30 minutes after he had loss of consciousness. His wife 
says he was making dinner when he fell on the kitchen floor. He has hypertension and his only medication is lisinopril. He has no history of operative procedures. En route, his temperature was 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse was 90/min, respirations were 10/min and labored, and blood pressure was 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 40% oxygen by face mask showed an oxygen saturation 
of 99%. On arrival, he is unresponsive. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 90/min, ventilatory rate is 10/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an F
IO2 of 0.4 shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. On examination, Doll's eye (oculocephalic) maneuver shows absent eye 
movements, and the corneal reflex is absent. On ice-water caloric testing, there is no nystagmus or deviation of the eyes toward the ear being irrigated. CT scan of the head shows a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. When the patient's wife is notified of the patient's condition, she says he wanted to be an organ donor but does not have an advance directive. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate next step in management?

question: A 36-year-old pregnant woman, G2P1, at 39 weeks' gestation is in active labor. She presents with regular, painful uterine contractions occurring every 2 to 3 minutes, each lasting approximately one minute, and reports no vaginal bleeding. Her medical and obstetric history is unremarkable. Vital signs are stable: temperature 37.0°C, pulse 84/min, blood pressure 100/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination is consistent with term gestation and is nontender. External fetal monitoring demonstrates a baseline heart rate of 150/min with moderate variability, spontaneous accelerations, and an absence of decelerations. Uterine contractions are regular. On pelvic examination, the cervix is 6 cm dilated and completely effaced, with the fetal vertex at 0 station; ultrasonography confirms cephalic presentation. Following artificial rupture of membranes, which reveals clear amniotic fluid, a repeat examination 30 minutes later shows cervical dilation of 9 cm and the vertex at +1 station. A fetal heart rate tracing is provided. What is the most appropriate subsequent management?

options.A: Advising the patient to begin pushing

original_question:
A 36-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 39 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. She reports painful contractions 
that occur every 2 to 3 minutes and last 60 seconds. She has not had vaginal bleeding. She has no history of serious illness, and 
pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/70 
mm Hg. The abdomen is nontender and consistent in size with a 39-week gestation. External fetal monitoring shows a heart rate 
of 150/min with moderate variability, several spontaneous accelerations, and no decelerations. An external tocometer shows 
regular uterine contractions every 2 to 3 minutes. The cervix is 6 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at 0 station. 
Ultrasonography shows the fetus in a cephalic presentation. The membranes are artificially ruptured, yielding copious clear fluid. 
Thirty minutes later, the cervix is 9 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at +1 station. A fetal heart tracing is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 27-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of productive cough, nasal congestion, intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate cheek pain. She reports experiencing similar episodes 3-4 times per year for the past 5 years, with each episode lasting 2 to 8 weeks before resolving on its own. Her history includes two prior episodes of bacterial pneumonia in the last 5 years, both successfully treated with antibiotics. She currently takes only acetaminophen as needed. Vital signs and physical examination are unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal decreased levels of IgA, IgG, and IgM. Following the initiation of antibiotic treatment, her symptoms improve. Which of the following interventions is most likely to reduce the risk of recurrent infections?
original_question:
A 27-year-old w oman comes to the office because of a 3-week history of nasal congestion, cough productive of sputum, 
intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate pain over her cheeks. She has had similar symptoms three to four times yearly during the past 5 years. The symptoms last 2 to 8 weeks and generally resolve spontaneously. She has had two episodes of bacterial pneumonia during the past 5 years; both episodes resolved with antibiotic therapy. Her only current 
medication is acetaminophen as needed for headache. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum study results show decreased IgA, IgG, and IgM concentrations. Antibiotic therapy is begun, and the patient’s condition improves. Which of the following is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection in this patient?

question:
A 37-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of intermittent mild to moderate fatigue, unrelated to activity level. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and consumes three glasses of whiskey per week. He has had two lifetime female sexual partners and consistently uses condoms. He emigrated from China 15 years ago. Vital signs and physical examination are unremarkable. Laboratory results are as follows:

- Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL  
- ALT: 85 U/L  
- AST: 60 U/L  
- HBsAg: positive  
- Anti-HCV: negative  
- Anti-HBs: negative  
- IgG anti-HBc: positive  
- Hematocrit: 35%  
- Hemoglobin: 12 g/dL  
- MCV: 82 µm³  

If his current condition remains untreated, which of the following is he most at risk of developing?
original_question:
A 37-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-year history of mild to moderate fatigue. The fatigue waxes and wanes 
without relation to activity. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He drinks three glasses of whiskey 
weekly. He has had two lifetime female sexual partners; he uses condoms consistently. He immigrated to the United States from 
China 15 years ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies 
are shown: 
 
Serum    
 
  
  
 
 
 
  Creatinine  1.2 mg/dL  
ALT  85 U/L

question: A 10-year-old boy with a history of hemophilia A presents with a moderately painful right knee. The joint is warm, swollen, and tender, with painful, limited flexion. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
original_question:
A 10-year-old boy is brought to the office because of moderate right knee pain. He is in no distress. Medical history is remarkable 
for hemophilia A. Examination of the right knee shows warmth and swelling; there is pain with flexion. Range of motion is limited by pain and swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy for this patient?

question: A 72-year-old male presents with a 3-week history of visual distortion in his right eye, describing straight lines such as those in crossword puzzles or window blinds as curved or wavy. He uses reading glasses but denies eye pain, photophobia, or prior trauma. Visual acuity is 20/200 in the right eye and 20/100 in the left. Pupils are round and reactive, globes are non-tender to palpation, red reflex is intact bilaterally, and lenses are clear. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-week history of visual changes in his right eye. During this period, he has 
noticed that the lines of his daily crossword puzzle look curved, and the blinds in his apartment appear wavy. He wears magnifying 
lenses for reading. He has not had pain in his eye or photophobia. He has no history of trauma to the area. On examination, visual 
acuity is 20/200 in the right eye and 20/100 in the left eye. Both pupils are round and reactive to light. Palpation of both globes 
through closed eyelids shows no abnormalities. There is normal red reflex bilaterally. The lenses appear clear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 28-year-old male presents with a 2-day history of a severely painful, erythematous lesion on his left forearm. He reports that while dressing in the dark two days ago, he experienced an intense stinging pain, causing him to drop his shirt, after which he observed a spider fleeing. He recently completed a one-week visit to the southeastern U.S. mountains. His medical history includes type 1 diabetes mellitus, managed with insulin and lisinopril. He denies alcohol use and reports having two male sexual partners with inconsistent condom use. Vital signs are normal. A photograph of the lesion is provided, and the remainder of the examination is unremarkable. What physical finding is most likely to develop over the next 24 hours?

options.A: Areas of  eschar at the center of the wound site
options.B: Gangrene of the distal aspect of the left extremity
options.D: Palpable purpuric lesions across the trunk and upper extremities
options.E: Rapidly spreading erythema around the wound site
options.F: Tender, erythematous streaks up to the epitrochlear nodes

answer: Areas of  eschar at the center of the wound site
original_question:
A 28-year-ol d man comes to the clinic because of a 2-day history of a red, severely painful lesion on his left forearm. He says that 
he felt stinging pain as he put his shirt on in the dark 2 days ago; the pain was so severe he immediately threw down the shirt and 
saw a spider running across the floor. He returned yesterday from a 1-week trip to the mountains in the southeastern United 
States. He has type 1 diabetes mellitus treated with lisinopril and insulin. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. He is sexually 
active with two male partners and uses condoms inconsistently. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the lesion 
is shown. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following physical findings is most likely to appear during the next 24 hours?

question: A 37-year-old man presents for a pre-employment physical and is asymptomatic. He has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. His height is 173 cm, weight is 100 kg (BMI 33 kg/m²), and his blood pressure today is 138/87 mm Hg, which is consistent with a reading of 136/85 mm Hg from one year ago. The physical examination is unremarkable. Beyond advising weight loss, what is the most suitable subsequent action to reduce this patient's risk of cardiovascular morbidity?
original_question:
A 37-year-old man comes to the clinic for a health maintenance examination prior to employment. He feels well. He has no history 
of serious illness and takes no medications. One year ago, his blood pressure was 136/85 mm Hg. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 100 kg (220 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m
2. Today, his blood pressure is 138/87 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. 
In addition to recommending weight loss, which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent cardiovascular morbidity in this patient?

question: A 39-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of fatigue, polyuria, and polydipsia. He reports an unintentional 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss despite a normal appetite. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination is unremarkable. His BMI is 19 kg/m². Laboratory studies are significant for a serum glucose of 578 mg/dL and a hemoglobin A1c of 10.7%. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management, following the initiation of dietary changes?
original_question:
A 39-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-week history of mild fatigue and increased thirst and urination. During this 
time, he also has had a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He is otherwise asymptomatic. He has no history 
of serious illness and takes no medications. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 61 kg (135 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Temperature 
is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 118/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no 
abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Serum    
 
  
  Na+ 135 mEq/L  
K+ 4.2 mEq/L  
Cl− 100 mEq/L  
Urea nitrogen  25 mg/dL  
Glucose  578 mg/dL  
Blood    
 Hemoglobin A 1c 10.7%  
 
In addition to recommending dietary modification, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: An 8-year-old girl is hospitalized after a motor vehicle accident with a femur fracture and closed head injury. She had a normal head CT on admission and no significant past medical history. Following open reduction and internal fixation, she experiences a generalized tonic-clonic seizure on the second day. Her only medication is acetaminophen for pain. On exam, she is drowsy but oriented, with stable vital signs and normal neurologic findings except for mild lethargy. Laboratory results reveal serum sodium of 122 mEq/L, with other electrolytes within normal ranges. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.A: Administer a bolus of intravenous 3% saline
options.D: Initiate therapy with 0.9% saline at 1.5 times the maintenance requirements

answer: Administer a bolus of intravenous 3% saline
original_question:
Two days after admission to the hospital for treatment of a fractured femur and closed head injury sustained in a motor vehicle 
collision, an 8-year-old girl has a brief, generalized tonic-clonic seizure. CT scan of the head on admission disclosed no abnormalities. She has no history of serious illness. Oral acetaminophen was initiated for pain, and the patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation of the fracture. She receives no routine medications. Today, she appears drowsy but responds to 
questions appropriately. She is at the 55th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 106/64 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation 
of 95%. Pupils are equal and reactive to light and accommodation. Ocular movements are full. Muscle strength is 5/5, and deep tendon reflexes are normal. Results of serum studies are shown: 
 
Na+ 122 mEq/L  
K+ 3.8 mEq/L  
Cl− 94 mEq/L  
HCO 3− 24 mEq/L  
Glucose  80 mg/dL  
Calcium  8.8 mg/dL  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 43-year-old man presents with two weeks of fatigue and mild right axillary discomfort, but no fever or weight loss. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypercholesterolemia, and his medications are metformin, simvastatin, and aspirin. His work involves frequent visits to homeless shelters. He has one male sexual partner and pets including two dogs and a cat. On examination, he is afebrile and appears well. There is a 4-cm, tender, mobile, nonfluctuant lymph node in the right axilla and a tender 1-cm epitrochlear lymph node. The abdomen is soft without hepatosplenomegaly. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 43-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-week history of fatigue and mild pain in his right armpit. He has not had 
fever or weight loss. Medical history is remarkable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypercholesterolemia. Medications are 
metformin, simvastatin, and aspirin. He is a social worker and frequently visits clients in homeless shelters. He lives alone with 
two dogs and a cat and has one male sexual partner. Vital signs are within normal limits. He appears well. Examination of the 
right upper extremity shows an erythematous axilla with a 4-cm, mobile, tender, nonfluctuant axillary lymph node and a tender, 
1-cm epitrochlear lymph node. Abdominal examination shows no hepatosplenomegaly. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 2-year-old girl presents for a routine health supervision visit. She was adopted two months prior and has an unremarkable past medical history and physical examination. Her parents inquire about the optimal time to discuss her adoption with her. What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.A: After she turns 18 years of age
options.B: As early as possible, even if she cannot process the whole experience
options.C: Once the seal on the adoption records has been lifted
options.D: Right before she is enrolled in kindergarten
options.E: When she learns that she is not biologically related to her parents

answer: As early as possible, even if she cannot process the whole experience
original_question:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the office by her parents for a well-child examination. She was adopted 2 months ago. She has no 
history of serious illness and receives no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. 
After the examination, the parents ask when they should tell their daughter about the adoption. Which of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding the best time to tell this patient?

question: An intensive care unit notes a rising incidence of bloodstream infections concurrent with longer durations of central venous catheter use. To address this performance issue, the unit's multidisciplinary team aims to reduce catheter dwell time. What is the most suitable immediate action?

options.A: Ask clinical providers in the unit to identify what they believe to be the most common factors leading to nonremoval of central venous catheters
options.B: Change all central venous catheters every 5 days
options.C: Place stickers on all intensive care unit doors reminding staff to reassess the n eed for a central line
options.D: Plan a randomized controlled trial to test the effects of a reminder to reassess the need for a central line on a daily goals sheet implemented during rounds

answer: Ask clinical providers in the unit to identify what they believe to be the most common factors leading to nonremoval of central venous catheters
original_question:
A surgical intensive care unit observes that the number of days that patients have central venous catheters in place and the number 
of bloodstream infections have been increasing. A multidisciplinary team from the unit would like to decrease the number of days that central venous catheters are in place in order to improve performance in this area. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

question: A 17-year-old female presents with a 3-month history of moderate epigastric discomfort that worsens following meals. She reports a brief, self-resolving episode of watery diarrhea approximately three weeks ago but denies any vomiting. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is on no medications. Vital signs and a physical examination, including abdominal assessment, are normal. Which of the following studies is most likely to establish the diagnosis?

options.C: Examination of the stool for ova and paras ites

original_question:
A 17-year-old girl comes to the office because of a 3-month history of daily moderate epigastric pain that is more severe after 
meals. Three weeks ago, she had watery diarrhea for 2 days, which resolved spontaneously. She has not had vomiting. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination, including abdominal examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

question:
A 23-year-old man presents to the emergency department with the acute onset of paroxysmal nonproductive coughing and associated dyspnea. The symptoms began one hour ago while he was cleaning mold in his garage using a combination of acetic acid and other cleaning products. His past medical history is noncontributory, and he reports no use of tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drugs. On examination, he is experiencing coughing spasms. His oxygen saturation is 94% on room air, and lung auscultation reveals diffuse bilateral wheezing. A chest x-ray demonstrates mild bilateral hyperinflation. An arterial blood gas on room air shows:
pH 7.41, PaCO2 38 mm Hg, PaO2 72 mm Hg, HCO3- 23 mEq/L.
What is the most suitable immediate management?
original_question:
A 23-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of intermittent coughing spasms that began 
suddenly while he was attempting to clean mold from his garage with a mixture of acetic acid and cleaning agents. The cough is 
not productive. He reports shortness of breath only during the coughing spasms. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no 
medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use other substances. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and 
weighs 75 kg (165 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen 
saturation of 94%. The patient has periodic paroxysmal coughing during the examination. Oropharynx is clear. Auscultation of 
the lungs discloses bilateral wheezes throughout all lung fields. There is no use of accessory muscles of respiration. The remainder 
of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Chest x-ray shows mild hyperexpansion of the lungs bilaterally. Results 
of arterial blood gas analysis on room air are shown: 
 
PO2 72 mm Hg  
PCO2 38 mm Hg  
pH 7.41 
HCO 3− 23 mEq/L  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?

question: An 87-year-old man with a history of T3N2 rectal cancer, status post low anterior resection 18 months ago and an ileotransverse bypass 6 months ago for a small-bowel obstruction due to diffuse carcinomatosis, presents with a 4-day history of abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting. He reports no flatus or bowel movements for 24 hours. His medical history is significant for metastatic colon cancer to the lungs, liver, and peritoneum, and his current medications include 5-fluorouracil and bevacizumab. On examination, his abdomen is distended with mild tenderness but no peritoneal signs. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.2°C, pulse of 100/min, and blood pressure of 130/80 mm Hg. An abdominal CT scan confirms a mid-jejunal small-bowel obstruction, ascites, and multiple peritoneal tumor implants. The medical team has recommended decompression with nasogastric and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes. However, the patient and his family are requesting a laparotomy with intestinal bypass, despite having been previously counseled that further surgery is not an option. What is the most suitable subsequent action in this patient's care?

options.A: Abide by the wishes of the patient and his family
options.C: Explain the futility of the operation to the patient and his family
options.D: Offer to transfer the patient to another facility

answer: Explain the futility of the operation to the patient and his family
original_question:
An 87-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 4-day history of moderate abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting. 
He has not had a bowel movement or passed gas in the past 24 hours. He has colon cancer metastatic to the lungs, liver, and peritoneum. Eighteen months ago, he underwent a low anterior resection for T3N2 rectal cancer. Six months ago, exploratory 
laparotomy showed diffuse carcinomatosis and bowel obstruction, and an ileotransverse bypass was performed. The patient and 
his family were informed of the findings and advised that he would not be a candidate for a laparotomy in the future because 
there would be no surgical options to treat another obstruction. The patient has no other history of serious illness. Medications 
are 5-fluorouracil and bevacizumab. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood 
pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and mildly tender to palpation. There are no peritoneal signs. CT scan of 
the abdomen shows a mid-jejunal small-bowel obstruction, multiple intraperitoneal tumor deposits, and ascites. The physician recommends insertion of nasogastric and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes. The patient and his family request that the 
patient undergo a laparotomy and intestinal bypass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 62-year-old woman is evaluated for a 3-month history of anxiety, insomnia, and increased frequency of bowel movements, accompanied by a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss. On examination, her pulse is 90/min with an irregularly irregular rhythm, respiratory rate is 22/min, and blood pressure is 150/65 mm Hg. She appears restless. The thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged with an audible bruit. Physical findings include palmar erythema, onycholysis, lid retraction, and pre tibial myxedema. A fine tremor is also noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 62-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of anxiety, insomnia, and frequent bowel movements. 
She has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss during this period. Pulse is 90/min and irregularly irregular, respirations are 22/min, and 
blood pressure is 150/65 mm Hg. She is restless. The thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged. A bruit is heard over the thyroid. 
Examination shows palmar erythema; some of her fingernails are separated from the nail beds. There is widening of the palpebral fissures and thickening of the skin over the dorsum of the feet with a peau d'orange appearance. There is a fine tremor. Which of 
the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 19-year-old male arrives at the emergency department via ambulance 15 minutes following a stab wound to the chest medial to the left nipple. Initial vital signs at the scene were pulse 105/min, respirations 18/min, blood pressure 120/80 mm Hg, and pulse oximetry 96% on room air. Two large-bore IVs were established and lactated Ringer's was administered during transport. Upon arrival in the trauma bay, his pulse is now 130/min and blood pressure is 80/40 mm Hg, with noted jugular venous distention. Given the findings from a focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST), what is the most appropriate subsequent intervention?

options.A: CT scan of the chest and abdomen with contrast
options.C: Needle decompression and chest tube placement

original_question:
A 19-year-old man is brought to the emergency department (ED) by ambulance 15 minutes after he was stabbed in the chest 
during a fight. The stab wound is located medially to the left nipple. At the scene, pulse was 105/min, respirations were 18/min, 
and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air showed an oxygen saturation of 96%. Two large-bore 
intravenous catheters were placed en route to the ED, and lactated Ringer solution was initiated. On arrival in the trauma bay, the 
patient develops a pulse of 130/min and blood pressure of 80/40 mm Hg. There is jugular venous distention. Results of focused 
assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST) are most likely to lead to which of the following next steps for this patient?

question: A 47-year-old woman with an unremarkable medical history is scheduled for right knee arthroscopy and partial medial meniscectomy. Preoperative evaluation and consent were completed two weeks prior. Upon arrival, paperwork is reviewed, and the patient is sedated in the operating room with spinal anesthesia initiated. After prepping the right lower extremity, a surgical pause is conducted to verify patient identity, consent, and operative site. The consent form cannot be located, and there are no site markings on the patient. The surgeon recalls the consent discussion and signature. The patient’s husband is available in the waiting area. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Ask the patient if she recalls signing the consent form and, if so, proceed with the operation
options.B: Ask the patient to sign a new consent form and proceed with the operation
options.C: Do not proceed, and remove the patient from the operating room
options.D: Obtain verbal consent from the patient's husband before proceeding with the operation
options.E: Proceed with the operative procedure as planned

answer: Do not proceed, and remove the patient from the operating room
original_question:
A 47-year-old woman is scheduled to undergo right knee arthroscopy and partial medial meniscectomy. Medical history is 
otherwise unremarkable. At her preoperative examination 2 weeks ago, her surgeon discussed the risks and benefits of the 
procedure, and both parties signed the operative consent form. On the patient's arrival at the hospital, the paperwork is reviewed. 
She is taken into the operating room and sedated, and the anesthesiology team starts a spinal anesthetic. After the patient's right 
lower extremity is prepped for the procedure, a surgical pause is taken to review the patient's name and consent form and to 
confirm the site of surgery. The operating room team is unable to locate the consent form for review. No markings are present on the patient's extremities to indicate the site of the procedure. The surgeon says that he remembers reviewing the consent form 
with the patient and seeing her sign it. The patient's husband is in the waiting room. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?

question: A 30-year-old man presents for an infertility evaluation after two years of unsuccessful attempts at conception with his partner. His wife's prior workup was unremarkable. He has no history of exposure to pesticides, heavy metals, radiation, or scrotal hyperthermia. The physical examination is normal. Laboratory testing reveals a serum testosterone level at the low end of the normal range. He is diagnosed with oligospermia. What is the most suitable next step in management?

options.A: Ask the patient about his alcohol intake, smoking, and stress level
options.B: Educate the patient about declining sperm counts with increased age

answer: Ask the patient about his alcohol intake, smoking, and stress level
original_question:
A 30-year-old man comes to the physician because he and his wife have been unable to conceive during the past 2 years. Previous 
evaluation of his wife showed no abnormalities. He has not been exposed to pesticides, heavy metals, radiation, or testicular 
overheating. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show a testosterone concentration within the lower reference range. A diagnosis of oligospermia is made. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial action by the physician?

question: A 40-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of fever, myalgias, fatigue, and pharyngitis. He has no known sick contacts and his medical history is noncontributory. He is not taking any medications and received the current season's influenza vaccine. The patient returned 10 days ago from a vacation in Malaysia, where he reports participating in local dining and entertainment. On examination, his temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F). Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin of 10 g/dL, MCV of 90 μm³, a leukocyte count of 2400/mm³ with a differential showing 60% segmented neutrophils, 5% band forms, 5% lymphocytes, 20% monocytes, 4% eosinophils, and 6% basophils. The platelet count is 350,000/mm³ and the red cell distribution width is 14%. A Monospot test is negative. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.D: HIV RNA polymerase chain reaction testing
options.E: Serum hepatitis B surface antigen testing

answer: HIV RNA polymerase chain reaction testing
original_question:
A 40-year-old man comes to the office during the summer because of a 3-day history of fever, diffuse muscle aches, fatigue, and 
sore throat. He has had no sick contacts. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. He received the influenza 
virus vaccine this year. Ten days ago, he returned from a trip to Malaysia, where he says he enjoyed the local food and nightlife. Temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 126/70 mm Hg. The remainder 
of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:

Monospot test result is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

question:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being found unresponsive. According to his girlfriend, he was in his usual state of health the day prior. His medical history includes polysubstance use involving cocaine, heroin, and prescription narcotic analgesics obtained from acquaintances. Upon examination, the patient is lethargic. Vital signs are: temperature 35.0°C (95.0°F), pulse 68/min, respirations 8/min, and blood pressure 96/50 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. The left leg is cyanotic and cool. The rest of the physical exam is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal the following values:

Potassium: 5.5 mEq/L
Bicarbonate: 18 mEq/L
Creatinine: 1 mg/dL
Creatine kinase: 50,000 U/L

Which of the following conditions is the most significant risk for this patient?
original_question:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his girlfriend 30 minutes after she found him unconscious in his 
home. She last saw him yesterday and he appeared well. He has a history of cocaine and heroin use, and also use of prescription 
narcotic analgesics that he obtains from friends. On arrival, he is lethargic. Temperature is 35.0°C (95.0°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 8/min, and blood pressure is 96/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96%. The 
left lower extremity is cyanotic and cold to the touch. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of serum studies are shown: 
 
K+ 5.5 mEq/L  
HCO 3− 18 mEq/L  
Creatinine  1 mg/dL  
Creatine kinase  50,000 U/L  
 
This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following conditions?

question: A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with progressive confusion over three days. She has a long-standing history of bipolar disorder, which has been stable on an unknown medication for the past five years. Her husband notes recent mild headaches treated with acetaminophen and reports increased outdoor tennis activity during sunny, warm weather over the last two days. In the ED, she experiences a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Vital signs show tachycardia and mild hypertension, with findings of dry mucous membranes on exam. An ECG reveals a second-degree atrioventricular block. Which of the following is the most probable cause of her altered mental status?
original_question:
A 62-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department (ED) by her husband because of a 3-day history of increasing 
confusion. She has a 20-year history of bipolar disorder well controlled with medication for the past 5 years. Her husband forgot 
to bring her medication bottles and cannot remember which medication she takes. He reports that she has recently had mild 
headaches and has taken acetaminophen as needed. On questioning, he says that there has been no particular change in their 
routine, but during the past 2 days they have been outside playing tennis more than usual because of the onset of sunny, warm 
weather. While in the ED, the patient has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m
2. Pulse is 104/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. 
Examination shows dry mucous membranes. ECG shows a second-degree atrioventricular block. Which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's confusion?

question: A 32-year-old woman with no prior pregnancies presents for evaluation after 12 months of unsuccessful attempts to conceive. She reports regular unprotected intercourse three times per week. Her medical history is notable for a motor vehicle accident two years ago, at which time a CT scan revealed an absent left kidney. She is otherwise healthy, takes only a prenatal vitamin, and has no allergies. Vital signs and pelvic examination are unremarkable. A hysterosalpingogram is provided. If she achieves pregnancy, which complication is she most likely to experience?
original_question:
A 32-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because she has been unable to conceive during the past 12 months. She 
and her husband have unprotected sexual intercourse three times weekly. Two years ago, she was involved in a motor vehicle collision; CT scan at that time showed absence of the left kidney. She has no other history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She has no known allergies. Vital signs are within normal limits. Pelvic examination shows no 
abnormalities. Hysterosalpingography is shown. When this patient conceives, she is at greatest risk for which of the following pregnancy complications?

question: A 14-year-old girl presents with a 1-week history of light-headedness and near-syncope, including an episode of tunnel vision upon standing this morning. question: A 47-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, gout, and a mechanical mitral valve replacement performed five years ago is seen for follow-up. She was treated in the emergency department three days prior for cellulitis of the right lower leg with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, which she has tolerated. With leg elevation, the cellulitis has shown moderate improvement. Her medications include lisinopril, allopurinol, and warfarin. Vital signs are temperature 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse 72/min, and blood pressure 128/83 mm Hg. Examination reveals a 4 x 5-cm erythematous, warm rash on the right lower extremity without streaking, vesicles, or purulence. Distal pulses are intact. Cardiac exam demonstrates a mechanical S1 click and a grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur loudest at the apex. Laboratory results are hematocrit 35%, WBC 13,000/mm³, platelet count 357,000/mm³, PTT 38 sec, and PT 57 sec (INR=5.2). Which of the following interventions would most likely have averted the current clinical issue?

options.D: Venous duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity

original_question:

department because of cellulitis of her right lower extremity, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy was begun. She has had no adverse reaction to the medication. She has kept her leg elevated, and her cellulitis has improved moderately. She also has hypertension, gout, and congenital heart disease. Her other medications are lisinopril, allopurinol, and warfarin. Five years 
ago, she underwent mechanical mitral valve replacement. Temperature today is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 128/83 mm Hg. Examination shows a 4 × 5-cm, erythematous, warm rash over the right lower extremity. There is no 
red streaking, vesicles, or purulence. There is no rash elsewhere. Pulses in the lower extremities are intact. Lungs are clear to 
auscultation. On cardiac examination, a mechanical click is heard during S
1. A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the 
apex. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:

   Hematocrit  35% 
WBC  13,000/mm3 
Platelet count  357,000/mm3 
PTT 38 sec  
PT 57 sec (INR=5.2)  
 
Which of the following actions is most likely to have prevented the near miss in this patient?

question:
A 58-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus presents for a routine visit. Her glycemic control has worsened over the past three months, with fingerstick glucose readings now between 80 and 230 mg/dL, compared to a previous range of 80 to 140 mg/dL. Her current medications are metformin and aspirin. She reports increasing her exercise frequency from once to three times per week and maintains a balanced diet, yet she has not lost weight. Her height is 157 cm, weight is 72 kg (BMI 29 kg/m²). Physical examination reveals a 1 x 2 cm, erythematous, soft lesion on the plantar surface of the left heel. There is diminished sensation to pinprick and light touch in a stocking distribution up to the ankles. Her hemoglobin A1c is 7%. Based on this presentation, which of the following is the most likely pattern of findings?

Glucose | Insulin | Insulin Receptor Responsiveness
original_question:
A 58-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine examination. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. During the past 3 months, 
her fingerstick blood glucose concentrations have ranged from 80 mg/dL to 230 mg/dL; previously, they ranged from 80 mg/dL to 140 mg/dL. Medications are metformin and aspirin. During the past 3 months, she has exercised three times weekly; previously, 
she exercised once weekly. She follows a balanced diet. Despite exercise and diet, she has not had weight loss. She is 157 cm (5 
ft 2 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m
2. Examination shows a 1 × 2-cm, erythematous, soft lesion over the 
posterior plantar aspect of the left foot. Sensation to pinprick and touch is decreased over the ankles and feet. Hemoglobin A 1c is 
7%. Which of the following findings are most likely to be present in this patient? 
 
     
 Glucose  Insulin  Insulin Receptor  
Responsiveness

question: A 39-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-hour history of moderate pain and swelling in his right knee and ankle. The onset of symptoms occurred after scaling a wall during an obstacle course, where he landed awkwardly on his right leg, rolled his ankle, and felt his knee buckle. He was able to limp to the finish line. His medical history includes right anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction 15 years ago. He is engaged to his boyfriend and reports feeling safe at home. On examination, he appears comfortable. The right knee demonstrates mild tenderness along the midsagittal joint line, with stable adduction and abduction stress testing at 0 and 30 degrees of flexion, full range of motion, and a negative Lachman test. The right ankle shows moderate tenderness over the distal fibula, ecchymosis on the anterolateral aspect, and swelling. There is no medial tenderness. Ankle range of motion is limited to 5 degrees of dorsiflexion and 30 degrees of plantar flexion due to pain. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.D: No diagnostic testing indicated  no diagnostic testing indicated

original_question:
A 39-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of moderate pain and swelling of his right 
knee and ankle. His symptoms began after he scaled a wall during an obstacle course. He landed awkwardly on his right leg and 
rolled his right ankle; his knee buckled. He limped to the finish line. He underwent reconstruction of a torn right anterior cruciate ligament 15 years ago. He and his boyfriend are engaged to be married. He feels safe at home. He does not appear to be in distress. 
Examination of the right knee shows mild tenderness to palpation of the midsagittal joint line. Adduction and abduction stress testing is stable at 0 and 30 degrees of flexion. Range of motion is full. Lachman test is negative. There is moderate tenderness 
to palpation of the distal aspect of the fibula. There are ecchymoses over the anterolateral aspect of the right ankle. The ankle is 
swollen. There is no tenderness to palpation of the medial aspect of the ankle. Range of motion of the ankle is limited to 5 degrees 
of dorsiflexion and 30 degrees of plantar flexion because of pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in 
diagnosis? 
 
 Knee  Ankle

question: A 13-year-old girl presents for assessment of short stature. Since infancy, her height and weight have remained below the 3rd percentile. She has not experienced menarche. Family history is negative for short stature, though her maternal uncle had delayed puberty onset at age 16. Her current height aligns with that of a 9.5-year-old girl. Vital signs show a pulse of 82/min and blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg. Sexual maturity is rated as stage 1 for breasts and stage 2 for pubic hair. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.A: Complete blood count and serum chemistry panel
options.B: Measurement of urine free cortisol concentration
options.C: X-rays of the left hand and wrist
options.D: X-rays of the long bones and spine

answer: X-rays of the left hand and wrist
original_question:
A 13-year-old girl is brought to the office for evaluation of short stature. Since birth, she has been below the 3rd percentile for 
height and at the 3rd percentile for weight. She has never had a menstrual period. There is no family history of short stature, but her maternal uncle did not begin puberty until the age of 16 years. Her current height is consistent with that of a 9½-year-old girl. Pulse is 82/min and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Sexual maturity rating is stage 1 for breast development and stage 2 for 
pubic hair development. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

question: A 20-year-old female presents with a mass on her left ear that developed approximately two months following an ear piercing one year ago. Physical examination reveals the findings shown in the image. What is the most appropriate first step in management for this patient?
original_question:
A 20-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-year history of a lump on her left ear that appeared 2 months after she 
had the ear pierced. Physical examination shows the findings in the photograph. Which of the following is the most appropriate 
initial treatment for this patient?

question:
A 42-year-old female presents to the emergency department via EMS 20 minutes following a head-on motor vehicle collision where she was the driver wearing a seat belt. She is alert and complains of chest and abdominal pain. Her past medical history is noncontributory, and she is on no medications. Vital signs show a temperature of 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse 120/min, respiratory rate 28/min, and blood pressure 130/80 mm Hg. On 4 L/min oxygen via nasal cannula, pulse oximetry reveals an oxygen saturation of 90%. Auscultation demonstrates diminished breath sounds on the right side. The trachea is midline. Cardiac examination is unremarkable. A seat belt-shaped abrasion with tenderness on palpation is noted over the right upper quadrant. Radial and dorsalis pedis pulses are intact bilaterally. Laboratory results are as follows:

Arterial blood gas on 4 L/min oxygen:
- PO₂: 65 mm Hg
- PCO₂: 32 mm Hg
- pH: 7.38

Blood:
- Hemoglobin: 10.0 g/dL

A chest x-ray has been obtained. In addition to providing intravenous analgesia, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
original_question:

in which she was the restrained driver. On arrival, she is alert and reports chest and abdominal pain. Medical history is 
unremarkable and she takes no medications. Temperature is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood 
pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 4 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 90%. Breath 
sounds are decreased on the right. Trachea is in the midline position. Cardiac examination discloses no abnormalities. An abrasion consistent in shape with a seat belt is present over the right upper abdominal quadrant; the area is tender to palpation. Radial and 
dorsalis pedis pulses are normal bilaterally. Results of laboratory studies are shown:

 PO2 65 mm Hg  
PCO2 32 mm Hg

question: A 31-year-old woman presents for evaluation of intermittent, nonbloody diarrhea that has persisted for three months. The onset of her symptoms occurred five days following a camping trip in rural Virginia, during which she consumed water from a spring-fed pond. Over the last three weeks, she has experienced a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss despite a normal appetite. She reports no fever or other gastrointestinal complaints. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She is sexually active with one female partner. On examination, her temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F). Abdominal exam reveals mild, diffuse tenderness to deep palpation and hyperactive bowel sounds. Stool is negative for occult blood. A stool smear has been obtained. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her infection?
original_question:
A 31-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of intermittent nonbloody diarrhea. The first episode 
began 5 days after a camping trip in rural Virginia with her family. During the trip, she drank from a spring-fed pond that she 
says was “clean.” During the past 3 weeks, she also has had a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. She has not 
had fever or other gastrointestinal symptoms. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is sexually active 
with one female partner. The patient is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (119 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.9°C 
(100.2°F), pulse is 85/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination 
shows mild, diffuse tenderness to deep palpation; bowel sounds are increased. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. 
Results of a stool smear are shown. Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?

question:
A clinical trial evaluates a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) for breast cancer prevention. The data from the study are as follows:

- **SERM Group:** 2400 participants, 7200 woman-years of follow-up, 7 cases of invasive breast cancer.
- **Placebo Group:** 1200 participants, 3600 woman-years of follow-up, 13 cases of invasive breast cancer.

Based on this information, what is the absolute risk reduction for invasive breast cancer, expressed per 1000 woman-years, associated with SERM treatment?
original_question:
A randomized placebo-controlled clinical trial is conducted to assess the effectiveness of a selective estrogen receptor modulator 
(SERM) for chemoprophylaxis of breast cancer. A subset of the results shows: 
 
  SERM  Placebo  
Number of participants  2400  1200  
Woman -years of follow -up 7200  3600  
Invasive breast cancer  7 13 
Breast cancer rate (per 1000 woman -years)  1 3.6 
Estrogen receptor status (invasive cancers)      
 Positive  2 10 
 Negative  4 2 
 Unknown  1 1 
 
Which of the following represents the absolute risk reduction for invasive breast cancer per 1000 woman-years in those treated 
with a SERM?

question: A 57-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-hour history of hematemesis, describing approximately 240 mL of bright red blood. Over the preceding month, she has experienced early satiety. There is no history of vomiting, weight loss, prior surgery, or recent use of NSAIDs or aspirin. Her medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She denies tobacco, alcohol, or substance use. Vital signs show tachycardia with a pulse of 116/min and a blood pressure of 110/78 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mild midepigastric tenderness without rebound. Stool is positive for occult blood. Laboratory studies show a hematocrit of 31%, a platelet count of 240,000/mm³, and a prothrombin time of 13 seconds (INR 1.2). After initiating intravenous fluids and performing nasogastric lavage, what is the most appropriate subsequent intervention?
original_question:
A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 1 hour after vomiting bright red blood. She thinks it was approximately 1 
cup of blood. She reports that during the past month, she has felt full sooner than usual while eating. She has not had other episodes of 
vomiting and has not had weight loss. She has no history of operative procedures. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. 
Medications are hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She has not taken aspirin or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the past 
month. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use other substances. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 116/min, 
respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Examination shows mild tenderness to palpation in the midepigastric 
area; there is no rebound tenderness. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Hematocrit is 31%, platelet count is 240,000/mm3, 
and prothrombin time is 13 seconds (INR=1.2).

In addition to intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, which of the following is the most appropriate 
next step in management?

question: A 57-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-hour history of vomiting approximately 240 mL of bright red blood. Over the past month, she has experienced early satiety. She denies other vomiting episodes, weight loss, surgical history, or recent use of aspirin or NSAIDs. Her medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She does not use tobacco, alcohol, or illicit substances. Vital signs show tachycardia with a pulse of 116/min and blood pressure of 110/78 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mild midepigastric tenderness without rebound. After fluid resuscitation and nasogastric lavage, esophagogastroduodenoscopy is performed, and an endoscopic image is provided. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
original_question:
A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 1 hour after vomiting bright red blood. She thinks it was approximately 1 
cup of blood. She reports that during the past month, she has felt full sooner than usual while eating. She has not had other episodes of 
vomiting and has not had weight loss. She has no history of operative procedures. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. 
Medications are hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She has not taken aspirin or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the past 
month. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use other substances. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 116/min, 
respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Examination shows mild tenderness to palpation in the midepigastric 
area; there is no rebound tenderness. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Hematocrit is 31%, platelet count is 240,000/mm3, 
and prothrombin time is 13 seconds (INR=1.2).

After intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, the patient undergoes esophagogastroduodenoscopy. A 
photograph of the endoscopic findings is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 77-year-old female resident of a skilled nursing facility is evaluated after staff report blood-stained bedding. She has a history of cerebral infarction 8 years prior resulting in left hemiparesis and chronic bedbound status. Her current medications include warfarin. Her height is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in), weight is 50 kg (110 lb), and BMI is 20 kg/m². Vital signs: temperature 36.0°C (96.8°F), pulse 70/min, respirations 18/min, blood pressure 180/90 mm Hg. No lymphadenopathy is noted. Physical exam reveals a small, clean sacral decubitus ulcer and a 3-cm raised, fleshy mass on the right labium majus. Vaginal examination is deferred due to patient discomfort. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

original_question:
A 77-year-old woman who resides in a skilled nursing care facility is examined by the physician 1 day after staff noticed some 
blood on her bedsheets. She sustained a cerebral infarction 8 years ago and has been confined to bed since then because of residual 
left hemiparesis. She is currently taking warfarin. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. 
Temperature is 36.0°C (96.8°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 180/90 mm Hg. There is no 
lymphadenopathy. Examination shows a small, clean, decubitus ulcer on the sacrum. Pelvic examination shows a 3-cm, raised, 
fleshy lesion on the right labium majus. Vaginal examination cannot be performed because of the patient's discomfort. Which of 
the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 68-year-old man presents with a three-month history of moderate low back pain radiating to both legs. The pain initially occurred while standing in line at a theater and was associated with bilateral leg tingling. Similar episodes have since occurred during prolonged standing, such as in a grocery checkout line. The pain does not respond to ibuprofen or naproxen but is relieved by sitting or lying down. His usual routine of walking four times weekly was discontinued two months ago due to symptom provocation. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and his medications are hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene, simvastatin, and aspirin. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history. Vital signs show a pulse of 74/min and blood pressure of 140/90 mm Hg. Lung examination reveals clear breath sounds with a prolonged expiratory phase. Cardiac examination demonstrates a grade 2/6 systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the upper right sternal border. There is trace bilateral ankle edema; the ankle-brachial index is 1.0. Musculoskeletal and neurologic examinations are unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 68-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of moderate low back pain that extends into both legs. He 
first noticed the pain while standing in the ticket line at a theater. At that time, the pain was accompanied by tingling in both legs. 
The symptoms recurred a few days later as he was standing in the grocery checkout line. The pain has not responded to ibuprofen 
or naproxen but improves with sitting or lying down. Prior to onset of the pain, the patient typically walked for 30 minutes four 
times weekly. He discontinued his walking regimen 2 months ago because it seemed to trigger his back pain. He has hypertension 
and hyperlipidemia. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene, simvastatin, and aspirin. The patient has smoked one pack 
of cigarettes daily for 40 years. Pulse is 74/min and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation; expiration 
phase is delayed. On cardiac examination, a grade 2/6 systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur is heard at the upper right sternal 
border. There is trace ankle edema bilaterally; ankle brachial index is 1.0. Musculoskeletal and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 50-year-old man with schizophrenia and emphysema presents with a 3-day history of headache and intermittent confusion. His medications include clozapine, sertraline, and inhaled beclomethasone with salmeterol, and he uses home oxygen. He is a former heavy smoker. Vital signs are normal, and the physical examination is notable only for moist mucous membranes and clear lungs. Laboratory studies reveal a sodium level of 116 mEq/L with other electrolytes, renal function, and glucose within normal limits. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Measurement of serum cortisol and TSH concentrations
options.C: Measurement of serum uric acid concentration
options.D: Measurement of urine and plasma osmolarity

answer: Measurement of urine and plasma osmolarity
original_question:
A 50-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-day history of mild headache and intermittent confusion. He has a 30-year 
history of schizophrenia and a 5-year history of emphysema. Medications are clozapine, sertraline, and inhaled beclomethasone 
and salmeterol. He also receives home oxygen therapy. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 33 years but quit 5 years ago. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), respirations are 20/min, pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Examination 
shows moist mucous membranes. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic examination shows 
no focal findings. Results of a complete blood count are within the reference ranges. Results of other laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Serum    
 
  
  
 Na+ 116 mEq/L  
K+ 4 mEq/L  
Cl− 90 mEq/L  
Urea nitrogen  10 mg/dL  
Creatinine  1 mg/dL  
Glucose  100 mg/dL  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 67-year-old man with end-stage pancreatic cancer is hospitalized for pneumonia and is now in the ICU. He is hypoventilating with a respiratory rate of 6/min and has an oxygen saturation of 78% despite 100% oxygen. His respiratory efforts are feeble, and he is only oriented to person. His wife states he recently expressed a wish to avoid mechanical ventilation, though no formal advance directive exists. His daughter demands that he be intubated. Without intervention, the patient will die within hours. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Perform endotracheal intubation and begin mechanical ventilation
options.B: Perform endotracheal intubation and then consult the hospital ethics committee regarding mechanical ventilation
options.E: Seek a court order to assign a legal guardian

original_question:
A 67-year-old man is evaluated in the intensive care unit. He has end-stage pancreatic cancer and was hospitalized 3 days ago for 
treatment of pneumonia. Respirations are 6/min. Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen by face mask shows an oxygen saturation of 78%. Examination shows feeble respiratory efforts; he is using accessory muscles of respiration. On mental status examination, 
the patient is oriented to person but not to place or time. If the patient is not endotracheally intubated and mechanically ventilated, he will die within hours. His wife says the patient recently told her that he would never want mechanical ventilation, but they never completed paperwork regarding his wishes. His daughter insists that he be mechanically ventilated. Which of the following 
is the most appropriate action for the physician to take?

question: A newborn boy, 5 days old, presents for his first well-child visit. He was delivered at full term and discharged home approximately 60 hours after birth. Newborn screening with hemoglobin electrophoresis revealed an FS pattern. His vital signs are normal, and his growth parameters are at the 50th percentile. The physical examination is unremarkable, and he appears healthy. What is the most appropriate management step at this time?
original_question:
A 5-day-old boy is brought to the office for an initial well-child examination. He was born at 40 weeks' gestation and discharged 
at 60 hours of life. On newborn screening, hemoglobin electrophoresis showed an FS pattern. He is at the 50th percentile for 
length and weight. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 136/min, and respirations are 34/min. He appears well. Examination 
shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 7-day-old female infant returns for a follow-up visit after her initial newborn screening revealed an elevated serum TSH level of 40 μU/mL (normal range 1–20). The infant is feeding well at the breast, producing five to six stools and multiple wet diapers per day, and has regained her birth weight. Vital signs and physical examination are unremarkable. Repeat laboratory results now show a TSH of 14 μU/mL (normal 0.5–6.5) and a free T4 of 0.4 ng/dL (normal 0.9–2.2). What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Monthly serial measurement of serum thyroglobulin concentration
options.D: Monthly serial measurement of serum TSH and free thyroxine (FT 4) concentrations

original_question:
A 1-week-old female newborn is brought to the office for a follow-up examination after newborn screening showed a serum TSH 
concentration of 40 μU/mL (N=1–20). She has been breast-feeding well. She passes five to six stools and has multiple wet diapers 
daily. She has returned to her birth weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Serum studies today show a TSH concentration of 14 μU/mL (N=0.5–6.5) and free thyroxine (FT
4) concentration of 0.4 ng/dL (N=0.9–
2.2). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: An 18-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of intense abdominal pain. The pain originated periumbilically following a large meal but has since shifted to the right lower quadrant. They also report a 2-month history of intermittent diarrhea. A self-resolved upper respiratory infection occurred three weeks prior. The patient is on no medications, does not smoke or use substances, and was assigned male at birth. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse 80/min, respirations 20/min, and blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg. On abdominal exam, there is fullness and tenderness in the right lower quadrant. The leukocyte count is 11,000/mm³. What is the most appropriate diagnostic step to take next?

options.A: Abdominal ultrasonography of the right lower quadrant
options.E: Upper gastrointestinal series with small bowel follow-through

original_question:
An 18-year-old patient comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of severe abdominal pain. The pain began 
in the middle of the abdomen after a large meal but now is localized to the right lower quadrant. The patient also has a 2-month 
history of intermittent diarrhea. Three weeks ago, the patient had an upper respiratory tract infection that resolved spontaneously. 
The patient currently takes no medications. The patient identifies as nonbinary and uses they/they pronouns; their assigned sex 
at birth was male. They do not smoke cigarettes or use other substances. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows fullness and tenderness to palpation 
of the right lower quadrant. Leukocyte count is 11,000/mm^3. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in 
diagnosis?

question: A 25-year-old pregnant woman, in her third pregnancy with two prior deliveries, presents at 36 weeks of gestation to the emergency department. The chief complaint is significant vaginal bleeding that began 3 hours after sexual intercourse. She reports having had no prenatal care during this pregnancy and has no significant past medical or surgical history. Her vital signs are stable. External fetal monitoring reveals a baseline fetal heart rate of 155 beats per minute, without accelerations or decelerations. On examination, the uterine size is appropriate for the gestational age. Tocodynamometry indicates uterine contractions occurring every 8 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
original_question:
A 25-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, at 36 weeks’ gestation comes to the emergency department because of heavy vaginal 
bleeding following sexual intercourse 3 hours ago. She has received no prenatal care. She has no history of serious illness or 
operative procedures. Vital signs are within normal limits. Continuous external fetal heart monitoring shows a baseline of 155/min with no accelerations or decelerations. The uterus is consistent in size with a 36-week gestation. Tocodynamometer monitoring 
shows contractions every 8 minutes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 75-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, COPD, and a prior stroke resulting in right lower extremity hemiparesis is hospitalized for a 3-day febrile illness. Following successful treatment, he is afebrile and alert. During discharge planning, the physical therapist recommends a nursing care facility for optimal care. The patient, while acknowledging this, expresses a preference to return home with visiting nurse support. Outside the room, his son, who holds power of attorney, insists to the physician, "Don't listen to him. He needs to be in a nursing home." What is the most appropriate next action?

options.A: Inform the son that the decision is the patient's to make, regardless of the medical team's recommendation
options.B: Reassure the son that the patient will be admitted to a nursing care facility in accordance with the physical therapist's recommendation
options.C: Refrain from making any decisions until a family meeting with the son and siblings is arranged to discuss the matter
options.D: Request psychiatric evaluation of the patient's mental competency
options.E: Request to review the legal documentation designating the son as having power of attorney

answer: Inform the son that the decision is the patient's to make, regardless of the medical team's recommendation
original_question:
A 75-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-day history of fever. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, 
and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. One year ago, he had a stroke with residual right lower extremity hemiparesis. 
Medications are amlodipine, tiotropium, metformin, glimepiride, lisinopril, and aspirin. He does not drink alcoholic beverages 
and has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years. The patient is widowed, lives alone, and has three adult children. He is 
not in acute distress. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), 
pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen 
saturation of 96%. Scattered end-expiratory wheezes are heard. There is dense hemiparesis of the right lower extremity. 
Appropriate therapy is initiated, and 3 days later, the patient's condition has improved. He is awake, alert, and conversing with one of his sons about current events. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Discharge plans are discussed with the patient, his son, the physician, and a physical therapist. The physical therapist says the 
patient would be best cared for in a nursing care facility. The patient acknowledges that a nursing facility would provide better care for him but says he would prefer to remain at home with a visiting nurse. Once outside the patient's room, his son says, "I 
have his power of attorney. Don't listen to him. He needs to be in a nursing home." Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

question: A 59-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency department with the acute onset of left-sided hemiparesis and lethargy. The neurological deficit was sudden, occurring one hour ago. His vital signs reveal significant hypertension. Physical exam confirms left facial weakness, flaccid paralysis of the left arm and leg with areflexia, and intact pupillary reflexes. A non-contrast head CT demonstrates a hyperdense lesion in the right putamen. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology?
original_question:
A 59-year -old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of weakness of his left arm and leg and 
mild headache. He says that, when he was gardening with his wife, he suddenly became unable to hold his gardening tool, lift his left arm, or walk. Two weeks ago, he had a nonproductive cough and nasal congestion that resolved spontaneously after 1 week. 
He has hypertension treated with lisinopril. On arrival, he is lethargic. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 97 kg (215 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m
2. Temperature is 36.6°C (97.9°F), pulse is 94/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 174/109 mm Hg. The 
pupils measure 3 mm and are reactive to light. Muscle movement is decreased over the left lower aspect of the face. Muscle 
strength is 0/5 in the left extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in the left extremities and 2+ in the right extremities. CT 
scan of the head shows a large area of increased attenuation in the right putamen region. Which of the following is the most likely 
cause of this patient’s condition?

question: A 24-year-old woman, pregnant for the first time, presents at 10 weeks' gestation for a scheduled suction curettage. An ultrasound performed two days prior revealed a fetus measuring 9 weeks in size with no heartbeat. She reports a self-resolved upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. Her history includes a diagnosis of herpes simplex virus type 2 one year ago, with her most recent outbreak occurring 6 months ago. She has no other significant medical history. Her height is 163 cm, and her pre-pregnancy weight was 82 kg, corresponding to a BMI of 31 kg/m²; she has gained 3.6 kg during the pregnancy. Vital signs are normal. The vulva is shaved and without lesions. The cervix appears normal. The uterus is non-tender and palpated to be the size of an 8-week gestation. A homogenous white-gray vaginal discharge is noted with a pH of 5.5. The procedure is performed using sterile instruments and povidone-iodine preparation. What is the most significant risk factor for a postoperative infection in this patient?

options.A: History of upper respiratory tract infection

original_question:
A 24-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks’ gestation comes to the office for a scheduled dilatation and suction curettage. 
Ultrasonography 2 days ago showed a fetus consistent in size with a 9-week gestation with absent cardiac activity. Two weeks 
ago, the patient had an upper respiratory tract infection that resolved spontaneously. One year ago, she was diagnosed with herpes 
simplex virus (HSV) 2; her last outbreak was 6 months ago. She has no other history of serious illness. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) 
tall. She weighed 82 kg (180 lb) prior to her pregnancy; BMI was 31 kg/m2. She has had a 3.6-kg (8-lb) weight gain during her 
pregnancy. Vital signs are within normal limits. The vulvar area is shaven; no lesions are noted. Examination shows a normal-appearing cervix. The uterus is nontender and consistent in size with an 8-week gestation. There is homogenous white-gray 
vaginal discharge; the pH of the discharge is 5.5. She undergoes dilatation and suction curettage with sterile instruments and 
povidone-iodine vaginal cleansing. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing risk factor for postoperative infection in 
this patient?

question: A 74-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of acute, severe pain in her left foot, which began suddenly and has limited her ability to walk. She has no history of trauma. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, managed with metformin, insulin glargine, hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. She usually wears compression stockings for bilateral lower extremity edema but has been unable to do so recently due to discomfort. Her occupation as a traveling minister requires prolonged daily sitting, often exceeding 4 hours in her vehicle. She reports a 30-year monogamous relationship with her spouse. Vital signs show a blood pressure of 146/88 mm Hg, with others normal. A photograph reveals changes localized to the left great toe; the remainder of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory results include hemoglobin A1c of 7.4%, serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, uric acid 7.8 mg/dL, and normal electrolytes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 74-year-old woman comes to the clinic 2 days after the sudden onset of severe left foot pain. She has had difficulty walking 
during this time and has been using a cane. She has not sustained trauma to the foot. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus and 
hypertension. Medications are metformin, insulin glargine, hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. She typically wears 
support stockings because of lower leg swelling, but the foot pain has made it difficult for her to wear them recently. She is a traveling pastor and spends more than 4 hours daily in her car. She has been monogamous with her wife for the past 30 years. 
Blood pressure is 146/88 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the left great toe is shown. No other abnormalities are noted. Hemoglobin A
1c is 7.4%, serum creatinine concentration is 1.4 mg/dL, and serum uric acid concentration 
is 7.8 mg/dL. Results of serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most 
likely diagnosis?

question: A 47-year-old man with long-standing type 1 diabetes mellitus presents for a pre-exercise evaluation. He is asymptomatic. Physical examination reveals a grade 1/6 early systolic ejection murmur, loudest at the left third intercostal space, and mildly diminished light touch sensation in the toes. During an exercise stress test, he develops 3-mm ST-segment depression in the early stages without associated chest pain. What is the most probable diagnosis?
original_question:
A 47-year-old man comes to the office for a routine examination before beginning an exercise program. He feels well. He has a 
25-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus. A grade 1/6, early peaking, systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the left third 
intercostal space. Sensation to light touch is mildly decreased over the toes. The remainder of the examination shows no 
abnormalities. An exercise stress test shows a 3-mm ST-segment depression early in the test. The test does not produce any pain. 
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 22-year-old man with no significant past medical history presents for a routine physical. He is asymptomatic and reports feeling well. He recently completed a 10-month military deployment to Southwest Asia, where he began smoking one pack of cigarettes per day. Since returning to the US two months ago, he has been drinking approximately six beers daily while socializing. His family history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus in his mother.
