Vital signs show a BMI of 22 kg/m² and consistent blood pressure readings of 156/95 mm Hg and 160/92 mm Hg, which he states were previously normal. The physical exam is otherwise unremarkable. What is the most probable etiology for his elevated blood pressure?
original_question:
A 22-year-old man comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. He feels well and has not noticed any health issues. 
He is in the US Army. Two months ago, he returned from a 10-month deployment to Southwest Asia, during which he started 
smoking one pack of cigarettes daily. The patient says he is happy to be home and has been celebrating with his friends and family. He has been consuming an average of six beers daily since returning to the United States. He has no history of major 
medical illness. He does not use any medications or other substances. His mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. The patient is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m
2. His first blood pressure reading is 156/95 mm Hg; repeat reading 
is 160/92 mm Hg. He says he remembers his blood pressure being normal when he had it checked in the past. Physical 
examination discloses no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's increased blood 
pressure?

question: A 32-year-old man presents for evaluation of a non-tender rash on his arms that has been present for three months. Over the last month, he reports an unintentional 3.2-kg weight loss while his appetite has remained stable. He has no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. On examination, his height is 175 cm and weight is 72 kg (BMI 23 kg/m²), with a pulse of 98/min and otherwise normal vital signs. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable. A photograph reveals the rash on his left arm, with comparable lesions observed on the right. What is the most probable etiology for these skin findings?
original_question:
A 32-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of a painless rash over his arms. During the past month, he 
also has had a 3.2-kg (7-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. 
He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (158 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Pulse is 98/min; other vital signs are within normal 
limits. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. A photograph of the left upper extremity is 
shown. Similar findings are noted over the right upper extremity. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's 
dermatologic findings?

question: A 42-year-old woman with no prior pregnancies presents for evaluation of hot flashes and palpitations that have persisted for 6 months. She reports her last menstrual period was 6 months ago. Her menstrual cycles were previously regular at 29-day intervals but became progressively less frequent over the last 2 years. Her surgical history includes an ovarian cystectomy for a benign teratoma 13 years ago. She took oral contraceptives for 12 years, discontinuing them 6 years ago when she had a tubal ligation. Her family history indicates her mother and sister experienced menopause at ages 47 and 49. She has a 26-year history of smoking one pack of cigarettes per day. She is in a monogamous sexual relationship. On examination, her height is 170 cm, weight is 86 kg (BMI 30 kg/m²), pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg. Pelvic and general physical examinations are unremarkable. What is the most appropriate diagnostic step to take next?

options.A: Measurement of serum follicle-stimulating hormone concentration
options.B: Measurement of serum luteinizing hormone concentration

answer: Measurement of serum follicle-stimulating hormone concentration
original_question:
A 42-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of hot flashes and intermittent palpitations. 
Her last menstrual period was 6 months ago. Menses previously occurred at regular 29-day intervals but gradually had occurred 
less frequently during the past 2 years. She underwent ovarian cystectomy 13 years ago for a small benign teratoma. She used an 
oral contraceptive for 12 years until tubal ligation 6 years ago. Her mother and sister had menopause at the ages of 47 and 49 
years, respectively. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 26 years. She is sexually active and monogamous 
with one male partner. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (191 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Pulse is 82/min and blood 
pressure is 130/85 mm Hg. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, discloses no abnormalities. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

question: A 47-year-old physician is conducting patient evaluations during an afternoon walk-in clinic. A colleague observes that the physician appears groggy, has slurred speech, and smells of alcohol. The colleague has never noted these findings in the physician before. Earlier that day, the physician attended a celebratory lunch for a staff member's graduation from nursing school. The physician is scheduled to work for the next four hours, and a physician assistant is present to assist with patient care. What is the most appropriate immediate action for the colleague to take?

options.A: Allow the physician to finish his time at the clinic, but raise concern about the episode at the next physicians' staff meeting
options.B: Ask the office administrator to cancel the remainder of the clinic hours
options.C: Ask the physician assist ant to examine the physician's patients and discharge them before the physician has a chance to examine them
options.D: Confront the physician and ask him to leave the clinic
options.E: Report the physician to the clinic administrator and conduct the remainder of the clinic hours without him
options.F: Report the physician to the state medical board

answer: Report the physician to the clinic administrator and conduct the remainder of the clinic hours without him
original_question:
A colleague of a 47-ye ar-old physician who is conducting patient evaluations during afternoon walk-in clinic hours notices that 
the physician appears groggy, has slurred speech, and smells of alcohol. Earlier in the day, the physician had attended a celebratory lunch for the graduation of one of the clinic's employees from nursing school. The colleague has not witnessed similar 
findings in the physician in the past. The physician is scheduled to work at the clinic for the next 4 hours. A physician assistant is present at the clinic and typically aids the physician with evaluating patients. Which of the following is the most appropriate action for the colleague to take at this time?

question: A 68-year-old woman with a history of major depressive disorder and osteoarthritis is seen for a follow-up visit. She had been treated for a suspected urinary tract infection one month prior with a 3-day course of cephalexin, following a urinalysis that was positive for protein. She now reports complete resolution of her dysuria and urinary frequency. Her current medications are venlafaxine and acetaminophen. Vital signs are within normal limits, and the physical examination is unremarkable. A repeat urinalysis shows: specific gravity 1.015, pH 6.0, and negative results for protein, blood, leukocyte esterase, and nitrite, with 3 WBCs/hpf and 1 RBC/hpf. What is the most suitable next diagnostic step?

options.B: 24-Hour urine collection for measurement of protein concentration

original_question:
A 68-year-old woman returns to the office for a follow-up examination. One month ago, urinalysis showed protein during work-
up of a suspected urinary tract infection. At that time, the patient was prescribed a 3-day course of oral cephalexin. Today, she 
says her previous pain on urination and urinary frequency have resolved. She has major depressive disorder and osteoarthritis. Current medications are venlafaxine and acetaminophen. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 66/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 122/66 mm Hg. The patient appears physically fit. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Repeat 
urinalysis is shown: 
 
Specific gravity  1.015 (N=1.003 –1.029)  
pH 6.0 (N=4.5 –7.8) 
Protein  negative  
Blood  negative  
Leukocyte esterase  negative  
Nitrite  negative  
WBCs  3/hpf  
RBCs  1/hpf  
 
 Which of the following is the most appropriate additional diagnostic study at this time?

question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of progressively worsening right lower leg pain, which began after a fall while camping. Over the past 12 hours, the pain has intensified. The injury resulted in a minor scratch below the knee. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and does not consume alcohol. Vital signs show tachycardia with a pulse of 110/min, respirations of 14/min, and a blood pressure of 105/60 mm Hg. The right lower extremity examination reveals edema, diffuse cellulitis, and hemorrhagic bullae extending from the knee to the ankle; the left leg is normal. Following the initiation of intravenous antibiotics and the administration of 2 liters of crystalloid fluid, what is the most appropriate subsequent intervention?

options.C: MRI of the right lower extremity

original_question:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of severe right lower leg pain since he fell in the woods 
during a camping trip 2 days ago. The pain has worsened during the past 12 hours. He sustained a small scratch below his knee 
as a result of the fall. He has no history of serious illness and uses no medications or other substances. He does not drink alcohol. 
Pulse is 110/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. Examination of the right lower extremity shows 
edema, diffuse cellulitis, and hemorrhagic bullae from the knee to the ankle. No abnormalities of the left lower extremity are 
noted. Peripheral intravenous antibiotic therapy and infusion of 2 L of crystalloid fluid are begun. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 27-year-old woman in her third pregnancy, with two prior deliveries, presents for her initial prenatal appointment at 12 weeks' gestation. Her first delivery occurred at 41 weeks with an infant weighing 4167 g, and her second at 37 weeks weighing 4309 g. She has no significant medical history and takes only a prenatal vitamin. There is a family history of stillbirth in her sister, and both parents have type 2 diabetes. She recently immigrated from Mexico eight months ago. Her height is 157 cm, and her prepregnancy weight was 95 kg, with a BMI of 38 kg/m². She has gained 3 kg during this pregnancy. Vital signs are pulse 80/min, respirations 14/min, and blood pressure 110/60 mm Hg. Bimanual exam and ultrasonography confirm a 12-week gestation. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Measurement of serum free thyroxine concentration

original_question:
A 27-year-old wo man, gravida 3, para 2, at 12 weeks' gestation comes to the office for her first prenatal visit. Her first child was 
delivered at 41 weeks' gestation and weighed 4167 g (9 lb 3 oz). Her second child was delivered at 37 weeks' gestation and 
weighed 4309 g (9 lb 8 oz). She has no history of serious illness and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Her sister has a 
history of delivering a stillborn infant. Her mother and father both have type 2 diabetes mellitus. Eight months ago, the patient immigrated to the United States from Mexico. The patient is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall. She weighed 95 kg (210 lb) prior to her 
pregnancy; BMI was 38 kg/m
2. She has had a 3-kg (6.6-lb) weight gain during her pregnancy. Pulse is 80/min, respirations are 
14/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Bimanual examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 12-week gestation. Ultrasonography confirms a 12-week gestation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question:
An 84-year-old woman presents for evaluation of a one-month history of generalized fatigue. She reports no fever, weight loss, or abdominal pain. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, which has been managed with long-acting insulin and metformin. She has a hemoglobin A1c of 6.7% from six months ago and subjectively reports feeling well, though she has missed several recent appointments without explanation. The patient's son moved in with her nine months ago following job loss; her husband died five years ago, and she has no other children. On examination, she is disheveled. Vital signs show a blood pressure of 160/94 mm Hg. She is alert and oriented with a flat affect and no deficits on mental status exam. Mucous membranes and skin are dry. A fasting serum glucose is 380 mg/dL. Urinalysis is notable for 4+ glucose and is negative for ketones, nitrites, and leukocyte esterase.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
An 84-year-old woman c omes to the office because of a 1-month history of malaise. She has not had fever, weight loss, or 
abdominal pain. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus well controlled with long-acting insulin and metformin. She has missed multiple 
appointments during the past 6 months with no reasons given. Her hemoglobin A 1c 6 months ago was 6.7%. She says she has 
been “feeling fine.” Her son moved back into her home 9 months ago because he had lost his job. The patient’s husband died 5 
years ago, and she has no other children. She appears unkempt. She is alert and fully oriented. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and 
weighs 77 kg (170 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Her pulse is 80/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 160/94 
mm Hg. Mucous membranes are dry; there is no thrush. The skin is dry and intact. The remainder of the physical examination 
shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows no memory deficit. She has a normal mood and flat affect. Her fasting 
serum glucose concentration is 380 mg/dL. Results of urinalysis are shown: 
 
Glucose  4+ 
Ketones  none  
Nitrites  none  
Leukocyte esteras e none  
 
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 15-year-old male presents with acute right-sided facial weakness that began upon waking 2 hours ago. For the past two weeks, he has reported intermittent headaches, photophobia, and mild-to-moderate neck pain. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes only occasional acetaminophen. He resides in New Jersey and has had no travel outside the state in the last six months. Vital signs are temperature 37.4°C, pulse 78/min, blood pressure 108/70 mm Hg. Physical exam reveals moderate pain and resistance with neck flexion. On neurologic examination, there is incomplete right eyelid closure, an inability to wrinkle the right forehead upon upward gaze, and severe weakness of the right corner of the mouth. Ocular motility is normal and there is no ptosis. The left side of the face is unaffected. Jaw, tongue, and palate strength are normal and symmetric, with no dysarthria. Limb strength is 5/5, and coordination, sensation, and gait are intact. Lumbar puncture shows an opening pressure of 180 mm H₂O. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis results are as follows: Glucose 60 mg/dL, total protein 52 mg/dL, WBC 85/mm³ (30% segmented neutrophils, 70% lymphocytes), RBC 1/mm³. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.A: Measurement of serum angiotensin-converting enzyme activity
options.B: Measurement of serum Lyme (Borreli a burgdorferi) antibody concentration
options.C: Polymerase chain reaction test for cytomegalovirus

answer: Measurement of serum Lyme (Borreli a burgdorferi) antibody concentration
original_question:
A 15-year-old boy is brought to the office 2 hours after he awoke with weakness of the right side of his face. He has a 2-week 
history of intermittent headache, sensitivity to light, and mild to moderate neck pain. He has no history of serious illness. His 
only medication is acetaminophen as needed. He lives in New Jersey and has not traveled outside of the state during the past 6 
months. Temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse is 78/min and regular, and blood pressure is 108/70 mm Hg. Flexion of the neck 
elicits moderate pain and resistance. The right eyelids do not close completely. Ocular movements are normal. There is no 
evidence of ptosis. The right side of the forehead does not wrinkle when the patient looks up. There is severe weakness of the right corner of the mouth. There are no abnormalities of the left side of the face. Muscle strength in the jaw, tongue, and palate 
is normal and symmetric. There is no dysarthria. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all extremities. Coordination and sensation are intact. Gait is normal. A lumbar puncture is performed, and opening pressure is 180 mm H
2O. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis results are 
shown: 
 
Glucose  60 mg/dL  
Total protein  52 mg/dL  
WBC  85/mm3 
 
 Segmented neutrophils  30% 
Lymphocytes  70% 
RBC  1/mm3 
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

question: A 2-year-old previously healthy girl presents with acute onset of loud breathing, a harsh cough, and hoarseness that began upon waking one hour ago. She had rhinorrhea the day prior. She is not on any medications. Vital signs show a temperature of 38.5°C, heart rate 120/min, respiratory rate 32/min, and blood pressure 105/65 mm Hg. Examination reveals moderate suprasternal and subcostal retractions with inspiratory stridor at rest. What is the most suitable initial pharmacologic treatment?
original_question:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department because of loud breathing, harsh cough, and a hoarse cry since she 
awoke 1 hour ago. Her parents state that yesterday she had a runny nose. Medical history is unremarkable and she receives no 
medications. Temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 32/min, and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. 
There are moderate suprasternal and subcostal retractions. Inspiratory stridor is heard at rest. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

question: A 28-year-old male is in the intensive care unit following a motor vehicle collision that resulted in a small-bowel resection with anastomosis and a right nephrectomy for renal laceration, as well as bilateral minimally displaced pubic rami fractures. The patient is intubated, sedated with propofol and morphine, and responsive only to painful stimuli. Vital signs include a pulse of 110/min, ventilator rate of 12/min, and blood pressure of 133/68 mm Hg. The abdomen is soft but distended, with a clean and intact surgical incision. Urine output, initially 40 mL/h and bloody, has decreased to 5 mL over the last hour despite intravenous 0.9% saline. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.A: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
options.B: Repeat surgical exploration of the abdomen
options.C: Transfusion of packed red blood cells

original_question:
A 28-year-old man is admitted to the intensive care unit 3 hours after undergoing resection and anastomosis of a small-bowel 
injury and nephrectomy because of a right renal laceration sustained in a motor vehicle collision. He also has bilateral, minimally displaced pubic rami fractures. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. He is receiving morphine and propofol. He responds 
only to painful stimuli. Pulse is 110/min, ventilatory rate is 12/min, and blood pressure is 133/68 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and soft, and the incision is clean, dry, and intact. Since the operation, urine output had been 40 mL/h and bloody. 
During the past hour, urine output has been 5 mL and bloody. In addition to intravenous administration of 0.9% saline, which of 
the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 15-year-old girl with cerebral palsy, situs inversus, and severe scoliosis who uses a wheelchair presents with a 3-day history of diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and vomiting. Due to the difficulty and discomfort of transferring her, the physician performs the examination while she remains in her wheelchair. Her vital signs show a temperature of 38.3°C (101.0°F), pulse of 110/min, and mild dehydration. The physician, having diagnosed gastroenteritis in four other patients with similar symptoms that day, attributes her tachycardia to fever and dehydration and makes the same diagnosis, prescribing ondansetron. Five hours later, she presents to the emergency department listless and barely responsive with a temperature of 38.9°C (102.0°F), pulse of 180/min, and hypotension. A CT scan reveals a volvulus requiring emergency resection of necrotic bowel. Which of the following is the most accurate characterization of the physician's error?
original_question:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to the office because of a 3-day history of  diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and vomiting. She has 
cerebral palsy, situs inversus, and severe scoliosis. She uses a wheelchair for ambulation. During previous visits, transferring this patient from her wheelchair to the examination table has been time-consuming and has caused the patient discomfort. Today, the patient appears uncomfortable. The physician decides to conduct the examination while the patient is sitting in her wheelchair. 
Temperature is 38.3°C (101.0°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 100%. Examination shows mildly dry mucous membranes. The physician evaluated four previous patients today who had vomiting and fever; he diagnosed gastroenteritis in all four patients. The physician attributes 
this patient's increased pulse to mild dehydration and fever, and he diagnoses this patient with gastroenteritis. The physician prescribes ondansetron and tells the patient to return if she has intractable vomiting or if her abdominal pain worsens. Five hours 
later, the patient is taken to the emergency department after her mother finds her listless and barely responsive. Temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F), pulse is 180/min, respirations are 35/min, and blood pressure is 70/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96%. CT scan of the abdomen shows volvulus, and emergency resection of necrotic bowel is done. 
Which of the following best describes the error that occurred?

question: A 27-year-old man with epilepsy maintained on levetiracetam presents with a one-month history of low mood, fatigue, anhedonia, and psychomotor retardation. He reports significant insomnia with prolonged sleep latency and impaired concentration. Physical examination is unremarkable. The mental status exam reveals a dysphoric and tearful affect without psychosis or suicidal thoughts. Which medication is the most suitable initial pharmacologic treatment?
original_question:
A 27-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-month history  of depressed mood and fatigue. He reports that it takes him 
at least 1 hour to fall asleep at night. He has had decreased concentration at work and decreased interest in socializing with his 
friends. He no longer exercises. He has epilepsy well controlled with levetiracetam. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a sad mood and is briefly tearful. He has no suicidal ideation, hallucinations, 
or delusions. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

question: A 23-year-old woman with no prior pregnancies presents for evaluation after one year of unsuccessful attempts to conceive despite regular unprotected intercourse. She has a history of depot medroxyprogesterone use before attempting conception. Her menstrual cycles are regular at 28 days, and she reports occasional mid-cycle pain consistent with mittelschmerz. Past medical history includes a ruptured appendix at age 17 and pelvic inflammatory disease at age 19. Cervical cytology at age 21 revealed a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion, which was normal on repeat testing one year later. She takes a prenatal vitamin and uses ibuprofen as needed for mid-cycle discomfort. Her height is 168 cm, weight 59 kg, and vital signs are normal. Physical examination is unremarkable. Hysterosalpingography is performed, and the images are reviewed. If no intervention is undertaken, what is the most probable outcome for this patient?

options.D: Successful pregnancy within the next year

original_question:
A 23-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because she has been unable to conceive during the past year. She and her 
husband have had regular unprotected intercourse during this time. The husband has no children. Prior to attempting to conceive, 
the patient regularly used depot medroxyprogesterone. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals with occasional mid-cycle 
abdominal pain previously diagnosed as mittelschmerz. She had a ruptured appendix at age 17 years and pelvic inflammatory 
disease at age 19 years. At age 21 years, cervical cytology showed a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion; repeat cervical 
cytology 1 year later showed no abnormalities. Current medications are a prenatal vitamin and occasional ibuprofen for mid-
cycle abdominal pain. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal 
limits. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Hysterosalpingography is done, and x-rays obtained during the procedure 
are shown. Without treatment, which of the following is the most likely clinical course for this patient?

question: A 50-year-old asymptomatic woman presents for a routine health evaluation. Her past medical history and family history are noncontributory, and she is not taking any medications. The physical examination, including a breast exam, is normal. A screening mammogram reveals a 1-cm cluster of 20 pleomorphic microcalcifications located at the 2-o'clock position in the right breast. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 50-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She feels well. Medical history is unremarkable 
and she takes no medications. There is no family history of serious illness. Physical examination, including breast examination, discloses no abnormalities. Mammography shows a cluster of 20 pleomorphic microcalcifications in a 1-cm area at the 2-o'clock 
position of the right breast. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: Which of the following interventions would most effectively increase the proportion of patients with asthma at this clinic who receive annual pulmonary function testing, as specified by the community's standard-of-care guidelines?

options.A: Annual chart reviews of patients with asthma and feedback to the physicians
options.C: Placement of flyers in the clinic waiting room that remind patients with asthma to undergo annual pulmonary function testing
options.D: Pulmonary function testing of all patients examined for respiratory symptoms
options.E: Pulmonary function testing of patients with asthma at each examination preceded by an exacerbation

original_question:
A community has created standard-of-care guidelines for ambulatory patients with several common diagnoses. The community's 
standard of care for patients with asthma is annual pulmonary function testing. Recent analysis of billing records from one clinic 
in this community shows that only 34% of patients with asthma at the clinic undergo pulmonary function testing. Enacting which 
of the following procedures is most likely to improve this clinic's adherence to the community's standard of care for patients with 
asthma?

question: A 27-year-old woman, G1P1, presents to the emergency department with a 1-day history of moderate right breast pain. She delivered a healthy newborn via cesarean section three weeks ago for breech presentation and is breastfeeding. The infant has had difficulty latching on the right side. She has no significant past medical history and takes only acetaminophen and a prenatal vitamin. Her temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F). Examination reveals a 5 × 2-cm wedge-shaped, erythematous, and tender area on the right breast; the left breast is normal. Her abdominal incision is healing well. After initiating antipyretic therapy, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Application of a breast binder on the right
options.D: Fine-needle aspiration of the erythem atous area

original_question:
A 27-y ear-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of moderate pain in her 
right breast. Three weeks ago, she underwent cesarean delivery of a healthy newborn at term because of breech presentation. She 
is breast-feeding, but the newborn is not latching properly on the right. She has no history of serious illness. Medications are 
acetaminophen for postoperative pain and a prenatal vitamin. Temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F). Examination of the right breast 
shows a 5 × 2-cm wedge-shaped area of erythema and moderate tenderness. The left breast is normal. Abdominal examination shows a well-healing surgical incision. In addition to beginning antipyretic therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate 
next step in management?

question: A 12-year-old girl presents for a routine visit and reports feeling well. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Menarche has not begun. Her growth parameters are at the 30th percentile for height and the 60th percentile for weight. Physical examination reveals no glandular breast tissue, with areolae that are flat against the chest wall, and an absence of pubic hair. The rest of the exam is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate diagnostic step to take next?

options.A: Measurement of serum follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone concentrations
options.B: Measurement of serum growth hormone and thyroxine concentrations
options.D: X-ray of the left hand and wrist to determine bone age
options.E: No additional diagnostic steps are indicated

answer: No additional diagnostic steps are indicated
original_question:
A 12-year-old girl is brought to the office for a well-child examination. She feels well. She has no history of serious illness and 
receives no medications. Menarche has not yet occurred. She is at the 30th percentile for height and 60th percentile for weight. 
Examination of the breasts shows no glandular tissue; the areolae follow the skin contours of the chest. There is no pubic hair. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

question: A 42-year-old woman presents for evaluation of recurrent light-headedness and brief (10-second) syncopal episodes occurring several times a week. The symptoms are relieved by eating. Over the past two months, she has gained 9 kg. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. Physical and neurological examinations are normal. Vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies reveal a serum glucose of 41 mg/dL, a C-peptide level of 0.5 ng/mL, and an insulin level of 80 μIU/mL. Which of the following is the most likely finding on an abdominal CT scan?

options.A: Duodenal mass with multiple liver metastases
options.B: Fluid-filled mass between the posterior aspect of the stomach and pancreas
options.C: Low-density mass in the head of the pancreas obstructing the main duct
options.D: Vascular mass in the neck of the pancreas

original_question:
A 42-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-month history of episodes of light-headedness and loss of consciousness 
for 10 seconds. The episodes occur two to three times weekly at work. She says her light-headedness resolves after she eats 
snacks. During this time, she also has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no 
medications. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. 
Physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. Serum glucose concentration is 41 mg/dL, serum C peptide concentration is 0.5 ng/mL (N=0.5–2.5), and serum insulin concentration is 80 μIU/mL (N=5–20). CT scan of 
the abdomen is most likely to show which of the following findings?

question: A 12-month-old female infant is evaluated for a 3-day history of fever with mild lethargy and reduced appetite. There is no history of cough, emesis, or diarrhea. She is not on any medications and is fully immunized for her age. Her growth parameters show height at the 35th percentile, weight at the 50th percentile, and head circumference at the 60th percentile. Her temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F); all other vital signs are normal. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. Urinalysis from a catheterized specimen reveals 20–30 white blood cells per high-power field and is positive for nitrites. Treatment with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is initiated. The following day, the urine culture returns with >100,000 colony-forming units/mL of Escherichia coli that is sensitive to the prescribed antibiotic. What is the most appropriate subsequent management?

options.D: Ultrasonography of the kidneys and bladder

answer: Ultrasonography of the kidneys and bladder
original_question:
A 12-month-old girl is brought to the office by her father because of a 3-day history of fever and mildly decreased appetite and 
activity. She has not had cough, vomiting, or diarrhea. She receives no medications. Vaccinations are up-to-date. She is at the 
35th percentile for height, 50th percentile for weight, and 60th percentile for head circumference. Temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F); other vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no other abnormalities. A catheter urine sample is 
obtained, and urinalysis shows 20–30 WBC/hpf and is positive for nitrites. Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy is begun. 
The next day, urine culture grows greater than 100,000 colonies/mL of Escherichia coli that is susceptible to trimethoprim-
sulfamethoxazole. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 32-year-old man presents for evaluation of a persistent, nonproductive cough lasting 2 weeks. The cough occurs in paroxysms and has recently been associated with bilateral chest pain and post-tussive emesis. His symptoms began following a self-limited upper respiratory illness characterized by rhinorrhea, sneezing, and malaise that resolved a week prior. He reports no fever or dyspnea. His medical history is unremarkable, and his only medication is over-the-counter cough drops. He drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and has one male partner with whom he uses condoms consistently. On examination, he is afebrile with stable vital signs and 98% oxygen saturation on room air. The physical exam, including the oropharynx, sinuses, and cardiopulmonary systems, is unremarkable except for observed coughing spells. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment?
original_question:
A 32-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-week history of persistent nonproductive cough occurring in short bursts. 
During the past week, he has had bilateral chest pain with severe coughing. He has vomited several times after coughing 
vigorously. He has not had shortness of breath or fever. Three weeks ago, he had a runny nose, sneezing, and mild malaise that resolved spontaneously after 1 week. Toward the end of that period, he noticed the onset of cough, which has since worsened. 
He has no history of serious illness, and his only medication is over-the-counter cough drops. He drinks 24 to 36 oz of beer weekly. He does not smoke cigarettes or use other substances. He is sexually active with one male partner; they use condoms consistently. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (190 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m
2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 
70/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. During the examination, the patient coughs several times. There is no sinus tenderness to palpation and no cervical lymphadenopathy. The oropharynx appears normal. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses no 
abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

question: A 16-year-old male presents to the emergency department with moderate left knee pain and superficial abrasions after a skateboarding accident 2 hours prior. He reports a previous injury to the same knee 6 months ago but states it had resolved fully. He is otherwise healthy and physically active. Past medical history is notable for an inherited EXT1 mutation, also present in his father. He is not on any medications. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the left knee without effusion or instability; he ambulates with a limp but can bear weight. Radiographs demonstrate bilateral 1 × 2-cm bony outgrowths arising from the proximal tibia near the physis, unchanged from imaging 6 months ago. What is the most appropriate management regarding activity and further evaluation?

options.A: Biopsy of the polyps and recommendation to bear weight as tolerated
options.B: Biopsy of the polyps and recommendation for no weight bearing
options.C: MRI of the knee and recommendation to bear weight as tolerated
options.D: MRI of the knee and recommendation for no weight bearing
options.E: Recommendation to bear weight as tolerated; no further workup needed
options.F: Recommendation for no weight bearing; no further workup needed

answer: Recommendation to bear weight as tolerated; no further workup needed
original_question:
A 16-year-old boy comes to the emergency department (ED) because of moderate left knee pain and abrasions sustained 2 hours 
ago in a skateboarding collision. He has a history of occasional injuries sustained while skateboarding, including an injury to the 
same knee 6 months ago. He says his knee had felt fine until today's injury, and he has been fit and active. The patient has an 
EXT1 mutation, which is also present in his father. The patient takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. 
Examination shows superficial abrasions over the anterolateral aspect of the left knee. No effusion or instability is noted in the 
knee, but there is tenderness to palpation. The patient walks with a limp but is able to bear weight on the leg. X-ray of the left knee shows two 1 × 2-cm bone polyps extending, one medially and one laterally, from the proximal tibia at the level of the closing 
physis; this is unchanged from an x-ray of the same knee obtained during his previous ED visit 6 months ago. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate recommendation for activity and additional workup?

question: A 70-year-old woman presents after experiencing three separate instances of bright red blood per rectum over four days, each preceded by a sudden urge to defecate. She reports no associated chest, abdominal, or rectal pain, palpitations, dizziness, lightheadedness, or melena. Her past medical history includes osteoarthritis managed with daily ibuprofen, and a colonoscopy performed eight years ago was unremarkable. She takes no other medications. Vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 37.0°C (98.6°F), respirations of 16/min, and minimal orthostatic changes in pulse and blood pressure. Physical examination reveals moist mucous membranes, a soft, nontender abdomen with normal bowel sounds, and a rectal exam that shows an empty vault with no masses. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 70-year-old woman comes to the office because of three episodes of rectal bleeding during the past 4 days. During each episode, 
she had the sudden urge to have a bowel movement and then passed a bright red, bloody stool. She has not had chest, abdominal, or rectal pain, palpitations, dizziness, light-headedness, or black stools. Her most recent colonoscopy 8 years ago showed no 
abnormalities. She has osteoarthritis treated with daily ibuprofen. She takes no other medications. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 69 kg (152 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m
2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), and respirations are 16/min. While sitting, her 
pulse is 80/min and blood pressure is 130/76 mm Hg. While standing, her pulse is 88/min and blood pressure is 126/74 mm Hg. 
On examination, mucous membranes are moist. The abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended. There is no rebound 
tenderness or guarding. Bowel sounds are normal. Rectal examination shows no stool in the vault; there are no masses. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 14-year-old adolescent male presents for evaluation due to a 6-month history of behavioral changes. The patient was recently detained by police for trespassing in an abandoned house with peers; no substance use was suspected. The officer noted evidence suggesting prior unauthorized entries. His mother reports that, until 6 months ago, he was respectful and maintained a close relationship with his family. Since associating with a new peer group, he has become argumentative—particularly regarding weekend curfews—and is often irritable at home. He communicates frequently with these friends via text messaging, shares inside jokes and nicknames, and discloses little about his activities outside the home. Medical history is unremarkable, physical examination is normal, and urine toxicology is negative. What is the most probable explanation for this patient's behavior?
original_question:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to the office because of a 6-month history of changes in behavior. Two days ago, the patient and his 
friends were detained by police for trespassing on abandoned property and entering an unoccupied house. No alcohol or other substances were involved. The police officer who brought the patient home reported that there was evidence indicating this had not been the first time the boys entered the property. The mother says her son was polite and close with his parents until 6 months 
ago when he began socializing with a different group of boys in his class. She says the patient is constantly exchanging text messages with his new friends. The group has inside jokes and nicknames for one another. Every weekend, the patient goes out with his friends and argues with his parents about his curfew. At home, he is irritable and shares few details about his outside 
activities with his parents. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Examination shows no abnormalities. Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s behavior?

question: A 20-year-old African American man presents with a 2-month history of fatigue, malaise, weight loss, decreased appetite, and a productive cough. He reports recent dyspnea on exertion, having previously been active on a track team. His past medical history includes childhood asthma. He currently works at a lumber mill feeding logs into a saw. Vital signs show a low-grade fever. Diffuse crackles are heard on lung auscultation. Chest radiograph reveals upper lung field predominant reticulonodular infiltrates. Pulmonary function tests demonstrate a mixed obstructive and restrictive pattern. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 20-year-old man comes to the office at the end of the summer because of a 2-month history of generalized malaise, fatigue, 
intermittent cough productive of whitish sputum, and decreased appetite. During this time, he has lost approximately 4 kg (10 
lb). The patient previously was on the college track team but now feels winded when he tries to go running. He has a history of 
childhood asthma. He currently takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use other 
substances. He identifies as African American. He has been working at a lumber mill for the summer, feeding logs into a saw. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 77 kg (170 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m
2. Temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 78/min, 
respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 114/70 mm Hg. Diffuse crackles are heard bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Chest x-ray shows a reticulonodular pattern that is more prominent in the upper lung fields. Results of pulmonary function tests show a mixed obstructive and restrictive pattern. Which of the following is the most likely 
diagnosis?

question: A 2-year-old girl, recently adopted from China, is presented for an initial evaluation. Her history includes an unrepaired cleft palate and ventricular septal defects. Over the previous 18 months, she has experienced two bouts of pneumonia and over ten episodes of otitis media requiring antibiotics. Her growth parameters are at the 10th percentile for length and 5th for weight. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals bilateral middle ear effusions, a cleft palate, and a grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur; the lungs are clear and there is no hepatosplenomegaly. A chest x-ray has been obtained. Which of the following white blood cell findings is most likely to be seen on her complete blood count?
original_question:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her parents for an initial examination. Medical records show that she has a cleft palate 
and ventriculoseptal defects that have not been repaired. During the past 18 months, she has had two episodes of pneumonia and at least 10 episodes of otitis media treated with antibiotic therapy. The patient’s parents recently adopted her from China. She is 
at the 10th percentile for length and 5th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows middle ear effusions bilaterally and a cleft palate. Lungs are clear to auscultation. A grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur is heard on cardiac examination. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Chest x-ray is shown. A complete blood count is most likely to show which of the 
following leukocyte findings in this patient?

question: A 5-year-old boy presents in July with a 2-day history of a pruritic rash. The eruption started as small erythematous papules on the right forearm and rapidly spread to involve the left cheek and right ankle on the same day. He is afebrile and has had no gastrointestinal symptoms. There is no similar illness in the household. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. The family recently changed their bath soap brand. They own three cats and a dog. The patient attended a park picnic three days prior to presentation. Vital signs are normal. He is alert and scratching his forearm. Physical exam reveals mild edema of the left cheek and a papular rash on the right forearm, left cheek, and right ankle, with no other findings. Which of the following exposures would avoidance most likely have prevented this condition?
original_question:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to the office by his parents in July because of a 2-day history of an itchy rash. The rash began as small 
red bumps on the right forearm and spread to the left cheek and right ankle within the same day. He has not had fever, vomiting, 
or diarrhea. No other family members have similar symptoms. Medical history is unremarkable and he receives no medications. 
The family switched to a new brand of bath soap last week. They have three pet cats and one dog. Three days ago, the family 
attended a picnic at a nearby park. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 90/min, and respirations are 20/min. The patient is 
alert and scratching his right forearm. Examination shows mild edema of the left cheek. A photograph of the right forearm is 
shown. Similar lesions are noted over the left cheek and right ankle. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Avoidance of which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient’s condition?

question: A 45-year-old man with a history of alcohol use disorder, sober for 1 year, presents with a 6-month history of insomnia, nervousness, and fatigue. He reports falling asleep quickly but experiencing restless sleep, followed by daytime tiredness, irritability, and mild neck pain upon waking. These symptoms began after he lost his long-term job 6 months ago; he expresses ongoing worry about finances and employment. He currently desires alcohol but remains abstinent. Vital signs and physical exam are normal; mental status exam reveals constricted and anxious affect. What is the most suitable pharmacologic intervention?
original_question:
A 45-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-month history of difficulty sleeping, nervousness, and fatigue. He goes to 
bed at 11 PM every night, falls asleep within 15 minutes, then sleeps “fitfully.” After he awakens, he has mild neck pain and feels 
tired, edgy, and tense during the rest of the day. The company where he worked for 27 years closed 6 months ago, and he has been unable to find another job. He says he worries about his finances and job prospects. Medical history is remarkable for alcohol use disorder. He has not drunk alcohol for 1 year. He says he is committed to being sober but recently has “wanted a drink.” He 
currently takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a constricted, anxious affect. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

question: A 56-year-old man is brought to the office by his spouse for evaluation of nocturnal restlessness over the past 6 months. His wife reports that he makes punching and kicking motions during sleep and has inadvertently struck her on two occasions. He has also started to shout and speak profusely while asleep. She describes episodes where he appears to be enacting his dreams, and on two instances, he awoke and was able to recall the dream's content. The patient is unaware of the events and reports feeling refreshed in the morning. His medical history is significant for constipation managed with polyethylene glycol, and he takes no other medications. Vital signs and physical examination are unremarkable. Which of the following conditions is this patient at highest risk for developing?
original_question:
A 56-year-old man comes to the clinic with his wife because of a 6-month history of restlessness while sleeping. The wife says 
he m otions as if he were throwing or kicking a ball and sometimes punches the pillow during the night; he has hit her twice. 
Recently, he began talking in his sleep and yelling expletives. Occasionally, he seems to be “acting out” a dream; twice, he has 
awakened and could tell his wife what the dream was about. Otherwise, he is unaware of these episodes and feels well rested in 
the mornings. He has a history of constipation treated with polyethylene glycol. He takes no other medications. Vital signs are 
within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. This patient is at greatest risk for developing which of the following?

question: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 4-week history of progressive dyspnea and lower extremity edema. He reports nocturnal dyspnea that awakens him and a 6-month history of exertional substernal chest pressure. He has no history of syncope, major illness, or medication use. Vital signs show tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypertension. Physical examination reveals severe diffuse gingivitis, bibasilar crackles, a prominent S3 gallop, and 3+ pitting edema. The ECG demonstrates sinus tachycardia. Which of the following substances is the most likely etiology?
original_question:
A 27-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 4-week history of progressive shortness of breath and 
swelling of the lower extremities. During this time, he also has had shortness of breath during the night while lying down. He 
also has a 6-month history of intermittent, moderate substernal chest pain and pressure while exercising. He has not had syncope. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 
24/min, and blood pressure is 145/92 mm Hg. Examination shows severe, diffuse gingivitis. Crackles are heard halfway up the 
posterior lung bases bilaterally. On cardiac examination, a prominent S
3 is heard. There is 3+ pitting edema of the lower 
extremities. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely substance used?

question: A 39-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of recurrent headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis, occurring two to three times per month. His medical history includes hypertension managed with a calcium channel blocker and an ACE inhibitor. His current blood pressure is 166/112 mm Hg. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. An abdominal CT scan reveals a 4-cm mass in the left adrenal gland. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: 24-Hour urine collection for measurement of vanillylmandelic acid and 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid
options.C: Laparoscopic left adrenalectomy and right adrenal biopsy
options.D: Measurement of plasma and urine catecholamine and metabolite concentrations

answer: Measurement of plasma and urine catecholamine and metabolite concentrations
original_question:
A 39-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of episodes of headaches, palpitations, and sweating. The 
episodes occur two to three times monthly. He has hypertension treated with a calcium-channel blocking agent and an ACE 
inhibitor. Blood pressure is 166/112 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. CT scan of the abdomen shows a 4-cm left adrenal mass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 41-year-old woman with a history of alcohol use disorder and three prior episodes of pancreatitis is ready for discharge following treatment for acute pancreatitis, having tolerated oral intake. She lacks stable housing. Which of the following interventions is most likely to decrease her risk of subsequent hospital readmissions?

options.B: Enrollment in a health insurance plan

original_question:
A 41-year-old woman is being prepared for discharge from the hospital after treatment for acute pancreatitis. She has tolerated 
oral fluids well for the past 12 hours. She has had three prior admissions for pancreatitis. Medical history also is remarkable for alcohol use disorder. The patient does not have secure housing. Which of the following recommendations is most likely to reduce 
this patient's risk for future hospital admissions?

question: A 55-year-old woman presents for a 3-month follow-up after an uncomplicated myocardial infarction in the left anterior descending artery territory. She has been enrolled in an outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program since hospital discharge 6 weeks ago, but her attendance has been inconsistent over the last month. She describes a 4-week history of depressed mood, anhedonia, decreased appetite, and insomnia. Her medical history includes hyperlipidemia and hypertension. Current medications are aspirin, atorvastatin, and metoprolol. She is 163 cm tall and weighs 68 kg (BMI 26 kg/m²). Vital signs are normal, lung auscultation is clear, cardiac exam shows regular rhythm, and there is no peripheral edema. Laboratory results are within normal limits. Which of the following best describes the impact of this patient's depression on her overall health?

options.A: It is likely to decrease her perceived quality of life but have no impact on her mortality
options.B: It is likely to double her risk for death during the next 3 months
options.C: It is likely to decrease her risk for ischemia because she is likely to rest and avoid socializing
options.D: It likely will have no significant effect on her physical health

answer: It is likely to double her risk for death during the next 3 months
original_question:
A 55-year-old woman comes to the office for a follow-up examination 3 months after sustaining an uncomplicated myocardial 
infarction in the distribution of the left anterior descending artery. She has participated in an outpatient rehabilitation program 
since discharge from the hospital 6 weeks ago, but her attendance in the program has become irregular during the past 4 weeks. When questioned about this, she reports a 4-week history of depressed mood, inability to enjoy activities she previously found 
pleasurable, loss of appetite, and insomnia. She also has hyperlipidemia and hypertension. Medications are aspirin, atorvastatin, and metoprolol. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m
2. Vital signs are within normal limits. 
Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a normal rhythm. There is no peripheral edema. Results of serum 
studies are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most likely effect of this patient's depression on her overall 
health?

question: An elderly man is hospitalized for aspiration pneumonia. For the past 5 years, he has experienced episodic dysphagia with regurgitation of undigested food and liquids, which was not relieved by antacids. He had been otherwise asymptomatic until his current illness. He has no significant past medical history and denies tobacco or alcohol use. On examination, his vital signs show a mild fever and tachypnea, and he has decreased breath sounds at the right lung base. What is the most probable etiology for his chronic dysphagia?
original_question:
A 78-year-old man is evaluated in the hospital following admission 8 hours ago for treatment of aspiration pneumonia. He has a 
5-year history of intermittent dysphagia resulting in spontaneous regurgitation of undigested food and liquids. Over-the-counter 
antacids had provided no improvement in his dysphagia. Until the development of his recent fever, chest pain, and shortness of 
breath, the patient had not had any other symptoms and had not sought medical care. He has no other history of serious illness. 
In the hospital, he has been receiving intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. Temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 100/65 mm Hg. He 
appears comfortable and is sitting upright in bed. Decreased breath sounds are heard in the right lower lung field. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's dysphagia?

question: A 60-year-old woman presents for a routine health maintenance visit. She reports feeling well, has no significant past medical history, and takes no medications. Her vital signs are normal. Examination reveals cerumen occluding both external auditory canals, limiting visualization of the tympanic membranes; the external ears are otherwise unremarkable. She states her hearing is unimpaired. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.A: Irrigation of the ear canals by the physician
options.B: Manual removal of the cerumen by an otorhinolaryngologist
options.C: Recommendation for daily use of cotton swabs
options.D: Recommendation for use of a ceruminolytic agent
options.E: No further management is indicated at this time

answer: No further management is indicated at this time
original_question:
A 60-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She says she feels well. She has no history of 
serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Observation of the tympanic membranes is limited 
because of cerumen filling both external auditory canals. The external ears appear normal. No other abnormalities are noted. On 
questioning, she says she hears well. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: A 37-year-old woman is hospitalized following a hysterectomy and oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. On postoperative day 4, she develops acute chest pain and hemoptysis. Her medications include oxycodone, prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin, and a multivitamin. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C, pulse 90/min, respirations 22/min, and blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg. A left-sided pleural friction rub is noted on examination. Chest imaging reveals a wedge-shaped, pleural-based opacity in the left lower lobe. CT angiography and lower extremity venous ultrasound are negative for pulmonary embolism and deep vein thrombosis, respectively. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A hospitalized 37-year-old woman has a 2-hour history of severe chest pain and blood-streaked sputum 4 days after undergoing 
hysterectomy and oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. Current medications are oxycodone, prophylactic low-molecular-weight 
heparin, and a multivitamin. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 
110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a left pleural rub. An x-ray and CT scan of the chest show a triangular pleural-based density 
in the lower lobe of the left lung. No pulmonary emboli are seen in the major branches. Venous duplex ultrasonography of the lower extremities shows no deep venous thrombosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 77-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe, constant abdominal pain and dizziness that began one hour ago. His medical history includes hypertension, managed with hydrochlorothiazide, and a 60-year history of smoking two packs of cigarettes daily. He is 175 cm tall and weighs 113 kg (BMI 37 kg/m²). Vital signs are: temperature 37.0°C, pulse 120/min, respirations 25/min, and blood pressure 80/60 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. On examination, he appears uncomfortable. The abdomen is mildly distended with decreased bowel sounds. Peripheral pulses are diminished, with 1+ groin pulses and non-palpable dorsalis pedis pulses. Laboratory studies show a hematocrit of 37% and a leukocyte count of 10,000/mm³; serum electrolytes, liver function tests, and amylase are normal. An abdominal x-ray is obtained. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 77-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of severe, constant abdominal pain and 
dizziness. He has hypertension well controlled with hydrochlorothiazide. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 60 
years. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 113 kg (250 lb); BMI is 37 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min, 
respirations are 25/min, and blood pressure is 80/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 93%. He appears uncomfortable. Abdominal examination shows mild distention; bowel sounds are decreased. Groin pulses are 1+. Dorsalis pedis pulses are not palpable. Hematocrit is 37% and leukocyte count is 10,000/mm
3. Serum electrolyte concentrations, 
liver function tests, and amylase activity are within the reference ranges. An x-ray of the abdomen is shown. Which of the 
following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 7-day-old male infant is assessed in the nursery 4 days following the surgical correction of a myelomeningocele. The infant was delivered vaginally without complications at 37 weeks' gestation to an 18-year-old primigravida. Prenatal ultrasound had identified the defect, and the repair was performed on the third day of life. His measurements are at the 5th percentile for length, 10th for weight, and 25th for head circumference. Vital signs are a temperature of 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse 138/min, and respiratory rate 42/min, with an oxygen saturation of 98% on room air. The infant is resting comfortably. On examination, the anterior fontanel is soft and flat. Red reflexes are present bilaterally. The Moro reflex is symmetric in the upper extremities; no movement is observed in the lower extremities. A cranial ultrasound reveals mild ventricular enlargement. Which of the following is the most likely associated finding?
original_question:
A 1-week-old male newborn is evaluated in the hospital nursery 4 days after undergoing operative repair of a myelomeningocele. 
He was born at 37 weeks' gestation via uncomplicated, spontaneous vaginal delivery to an 18-year-old primigravid woman. The 
defect was diagnosed prenatally by ultrasonography, and the repair was done 72 hours after delivery. He is at the 5th percentile 
for length, 10th percentile for weight, and 25th percentile for head circumference. Temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 
138/min, and respirations are 42/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. The newborn appears comfortable. Physical examination shows a soft, flat, anterior fontanel. Red reflexes are normal. Moro reflex is symmetric in the 
upper extremities; there is no movement in the lower extremities. Ultrasonography of the brain shows mildly enlarged ventricles. Which of the following is the most likely associated finding in this patient?

question: A randomized controlled trial evaluates a novel antiarrhythmic medication in 100 patients with recurrent ventricular tachycardia. Participants are randomly allocated to receive either the new drug or a placebo. The trial results indicate a statistically significant (p<0.05) reduction in recurrent ventricular tachycardia episodes in the drug group compared to the placebo group. Based on this finding, the investigators assert that the medication will reduce mortality due to cardiac arrhythmia. Which of the following is the most significant limitation that challenges this conclusion?
original_question: A randomized controlled trial is conducted to assess the effectiveness of a new antiarrhythmic drug in patients with recurrent ventricular tachycardia. A total of 100 participants with ventricular tachycardia are randomly assigned to receive either the new drug or a placebo. Results show that the new drug group has fewer episodes of recurrent ventricular tachycardia compared with the placebo group. The difference was found to be statistically significant (p<0.05). The investigators conclude that the new drug can be used to decrease mortality from cardiac arrhythmia. Which of the following factors most likely invalidates the author's conclusions?

question: A 42-year-old female presents with a two-week history of throat discomfort and neck swelling. She reports no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. On examination, she appears anxious. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), heart rate is 102 beats per minute, respiratory rate is 22 breaths per minute, and blood pressure is 146/82 mm Hg. The oropharynx appears normal without exudates. There is tenderness and swelling in the anterior neck, but no lymphadenopathy. Lung auscultation is clear. Laboratory results show a normal complete blood count, and chest radiography is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 42-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-week history of sore throat and fullness in her neck. She has no history 
of serious illness and takes no medications. She appears anxious. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 102/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 146/82 mm Hg. The oropharynx is clear, and there are no exudates. There is palpable fullness 
and tenderness of the anterior aspect of the neck. There is no lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Results of a complete blood count are within the reference ranges. Chest x-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 75-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-hour history of abrupt fever, chills, pleuritic chest pain, and a cough producing rust-colored sputum. His chest pain is severe, rated 8/10. Vital signs include a temperature of 38.9°C (102°F), pulse of 106/min, respiratory rate of 22/min, and blood pressure of 130/80 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. He is in moderate respiratory distress. Examination reveals splinting on the left side, with dullness to percussion and egophony over the left lower lobe. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis with a left shift. An arterial blood gas on room air demonstrates a PaO2 of 62 mm Hg. Chest x-ray confirms left lower lobe consolidation. After starting intravenous antibiotics and supplemental oxygen, he is found three hours later lying on his left side with worsened dyspnea. Upon being turned to his right side, his breathing improves rapidly. What is the most likely explanation for this immediate improvement?

options.C: Positionally impeded filling of the left ventricle
options.D: Positionally impeded movement of the diaphragm

original_question:
A 75 -year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his son 2  hours after the sudden onset of fever, chills, pleuritic 
chest pain, and cough productive of rust -colored sputum. He rates his chest pain as an 8  on a 10 -point scale. Temperature is 
38.9°C  (102°F), pulse is 106/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 130/80  mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 94% 
on room air. The patient appears to be in moderate respiratory distress. Physical examination shows splinting on the left sid e. 
There is dullness to percussion and egophony over the left lower lobe. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. 
Results of l aboratory studies are shown:  
 
Serum    
 Calcium  8.4 mg/dL  
 Urea nitrogen  18 mg/dL  
 Creatinine  1.4 mg/dL  
 Na+ 131 mEq/L  
 K+ 4.1 mEq/L  
 Cl− 108 mEq/L  
 HCO 3− 25 mEq/L  
Blood    
 Hematocrit  37% 
 Hemoglobin  12.4 g/dL  
 WBC  21,000/mm3 
  Neutrophils, segmented  79% 
  Neutrophils, bands  10% 
  Lymphocytes  11% 
 Platelet count  250,000/mm3 
    
Arterial blood gas analysis on room air:    
 pH 7.45 
 PCO2 45 mm Hg  
 PO2 62 mm Hg  
 HCO 3− 24 mEq/L  
 O2 saturation  95% 
 
Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. A chest x -ray s hows consolidation in the left lower 
lobe and no cardiomegaly. Intravenous antibiotics are administered, and the patient receives oxygen via nasal cannula. Three 
hours later, he is lying on his left side and has increased dyspnea; he is rolled to his right side and his symptoms improve 
within minutes. Which of the following best explains this improvement?

question: A 55-year-old woman presents for her first evaluation since entering menopause one year ago. She reports two episodes of vaginal spotting, each occurring one month apart. Her medical history includes diet-controlled diabetes mellitus and untreated hypertension diagnosed two years ago. She is a current smoker with a 30-pack-year history and has a BMI of 42 kg/m². Her physical examination is notable for a nontender, firm, smooth, and mobile uterus that is enlarged to the size of a 6-week pregnancy. All other findings are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study at this time?
original_question:
A 55 -year-old woman, gravida 4, para 4, comes to the office because of vaginal spotting that has occurred once monthly for 
the past 2 months. Menopause occurred 1 year ago, and this is the patient's first postmenopausal examination. Medical 
history is significant for elevated blood pressure readings during the past 2 years and diabetes mellitus for which she follo ws 
an 1800 -calorie diet. She takes no medicat ions. Her most recent cervical cytology and mammography obtained 1 year ago 
disclosed no abnormalitie s. Family history is significant for hypertension in her mother who died at age 58 years from an 
unknown cancer. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 30 years and drinks beer occasionally. BMI 
is 42 kg/m2. Temperature is 36.7°C  (98.0°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 136/84 mm Hg. 
Bimanual examination discloses a firm, smooth, mobile, nontender ut erus that is consistent in size with a 6 -week gestation. 
The remainder of the physical examination di scloses no abnormalities. Results of dipstick urinalysis are within the reference 
ranges; fingerstick hematocrit is 35% and 2 -hour postprandial serum glucose concentration is 188 mg/dL. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate diagnostic study at this time?

question: A 28-year-old woman presents for a new patient visit. She appears anxious and reports that for the past six weeks, she has experienced sudden, severe episodes of anxiety and shortness of breath approximately once or twice weekly. These episodes occur without an apparent trigger. She recently finalized a divorce after seven years of marriage. Based on this initial presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?
original_question:
A 28 -year-old woman comes to the health center for an initial visit. The patient appears somewhat anxious. After 
introductions she says, "Doctor, something has been happening to me for the past 6  weeks or so that has me a bit scared. 
About once or twice a week, and out of nowhere and for no reason, I have this sudden feeling of anxiety. It really gets sever e 
and I get short of breath. I just don't know what's going on." She has recently divorced her husband of 7  years. Given the 
evidence at this time, which  of the fol lowing is the most likely diagnosis?

question: A 71-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 27% one year ago) and a history of hypertension is admitted to the ICU for an acute exacerbation. She has been on home oxygen for three years and has a do-not-resuscitate order that does not address intubation. Her daughter holds power of attorney, but the patient has historically made her own decisions. Following intubation and a 4-day course of antibiotics, corticosteroids, and bronchodilators, she is extubated. Two hours later, she develops dyspnea and difficulty clearing secretions. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.0°C, pulse 98/min, respirations 25/min, blood pressure 168/98 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 89% on 6 L/min oxygen. She is alert, oriented, and able to converse. Arterial blood gas reveals a pH of 7.32, PCO2 of 55 mm Hg, and PO2 of 61 mm Hg. Noninvasive ventilation is attempted but the patient finds the mask uncomfortable and asks for its removal. She initially refuses reintubation. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Clearly state to the patient that if she again declines intubation when offered to her one more time, she will receive comfort care only
options.B: Contact th e patient's daughter to request additional information regarding the patient's previous feelings regarding intubation
options.C: Explain to the patient that because her advance directive is confusing, discussing her options would be helpful
options.E: Request psychiatric evaluation of the patient's capacity to make health care decisions

answer: Explain to the patient that because her advance directive is confusing, discussing her options would be helpful
original_question:
A 71 -year-old woman with a 15 -year history of severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is admitted to the 
intensive care unit for treatment of an acute COPD exacerbation. The patient has been receiving home oxygen therapy for the 
past 3 years; FEV 11 year ago was 27%. She has a do -not-resuscitate order stating that she  does not want cardiopulmonary 
resuscitation, with no specific comment on intubation. Her daughter, who is not present, has power of attorney for the 
patient's health care decisions,  although the patient has made all decisions in the past. Medical history also is significant for 
hypertension. Medications are lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide, as well as inhaled tiotropium, albuterol, and fluticasone. 
The patient is intubated and mechanical ventilation is begun. She is treated with antibiotics, corticostero ids, and nebulized 
bronchodilators. Her condition gradually improves and she is extubated 4 days later. Within 2 hours of extubation, the patien t 
becomes short of breath and has diff iculty clearing her secretions. She is not using accessory muscles of respiration. 
Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 25/min, and blood pressure is 168/98 mm Hg. Oxygen 
saturation is 89% on oxygen at 6 L/min. The patient is alert, fully oriented, and conversant. Results of arterial blood gas 
analys is are shown:  
 
PO2 61 mm Hg  
PCO2 55 mm Hg  
pH 7.32 
 
An attempt is made to manage the patient with noninvasive positive pressure ventilation, but the patient reports discomfort 
caused by the mask and requests that it be removed. When initially asked, she declines to be reintubated. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate next step in management?

question: Which of the following inclusion criteria would lead to the most efficient study design for a phase 3 trial evaluating a novel medication to prevent type 2 diabetes mellitus?

options.A: Patients aged 18 years and older who have a BMI of less than 23 kg/m2 and are smokers
options.B: Patients aged 18 years and older with no significant medical history
options.C: Patients aged 45 years and older with hyperlipidemia and central obesity
options.D: Patients aged 65 years and older with a hemoglobin A 1c of 6.5% or greater

answer: Patients aged 45 years and older with hyperlipidemia and central obesity
original_question:

the following inclusion criteria should be selected to design the most efficient study?

question: A 60-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease, hyperlipidemia, and COPD presents to the hospital 4 hours after waking with dyspnea, dizziness, and pleuritic chest pain. His medications include atorvastatin, low-dose aspirin, atenolol, albuterol, ipratropium, and home oxygen. He is a long-term smoker. On examination, he is tachycardic and tachypneic with hypoxia despite supplemental oxygen. He has a parasternal lift, jugular venous distension, wheezing, abdominal distention with a fluid wave and epigastric pulsation, hepatomegaly, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Chest x-ray and ECG are available. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's presentation?

options.A: External compression of the superior vena cava
options.B: Helicobacter pylori  infection with transmural ulceration and peritoneal leaking
options.C: Lactic acidosis caused by systemi c hypoperfusion
options.D: Pulmonary vasculature compromise and cor pulmonale
options.E: Widespread hepatocellular necrosis and parenchymal edema

answer: Pulmonary vasculature compromise and cor pulmonale

question: A 67-year-old male veteran is evaluated at a rehabilitation facility three days after an uncomplicated revision of a left transtibial amputation to a transfemoral amputation, performed due to dehiscence of the prior surgical site. His medications are dabigatran, lisinopril, metformin, and metoprolol. He is afebrile with stable vital signs and appears comfortable. The incision is healing well, and his fingerstick glucose is 120 mg/dL. Which of the following findings on physical exam would most likely limit his long-term capacity for prosthetic ambulation?

options.A: A 3 -cm-diameter blackened eschar on the right heel

answer: A 3 -cm-diameter blackened eschar on the right heel
