of a warm, tender, erythematous  lump in her right breast that has increased in size durin g the past week despite applied heat 
and cephalexin therapy. She has repeatedly pumped milk from that breast but her symptoms have not improved. The patient's 
pregnancy was complicated by preterm la bor, for which she was hospitalized for 1  week prior to giving birth via vaginal 
delivery at 32  weeks ' gestation. Medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She takes a prenatal vitamin and is allergic to 
sulfa -containing medications. On admission, the pat ient appears flushed. Temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), pul se is 114/min, 
respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 120/76  mm Hg. Physical examination discloses a warm, 10 ×  10-cm, 
erythematous, tender mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right brea st. The mass is not fluctuant. Several enlarged, tender 
lymph nodes are palpable in the right axilla.  Ultrasonography -guided aspiration of the mass is scheduled. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?

question: A 34-year-old man presents for evaluation after a pre-employment PPD test for his new job at a nursing home is positive with 15 mm of induration. His medical history is significant for three episodes of scabies in the last year. He was previously employed on an asparagus farm. A chest x-ray is normal. He lives with his 32-year-old wife and their six children, aged 16 months to 12 years. Isoniazid therapy is initiated for the patient. What is the most appropriate subsequent action?

options.A: Administer PPD skin tests to the whole family
options.C: Order sputum cultures and gastric washings for the whole family
options.D: Schedule another chest x -ray in 3 months
options.E: Start the patient's children on isoniazid therapy

answer: Administer PPD skin tests to the whole family
original_question:
A 34 -year-old man is referred to the office by his new employer because of a positive PPD skin test. Medical histor y is 
remarkable for three episodes of scabies during the past year. He previously harvested asparagus  at a local farm but says he 
recently acquired a position at the local nursing home; part of the required examination was to have the PPD skin test. His 
test result was positive with 15 mm of induration. Chest x -ray shows no abnormalities. His 32 -year-old wife and six children, 
who range in age from 12 years to 16 months, live in the same house. The patient is started on isoniazid therapy. Which of 
the follo wing is the most appropriate next step?

question: A 62-year-old African American man presents to the emergency department after a sudden syncopal episode at home. He has no memory of the event. His wife reports he collapsed and was found within a minute, quickly regaining consciousness. He now describes a constant, severe (10/10), crushing pain in his chest and back. His medical history is significant for hypertension managed with amlodipine. Vital signs reveal tachycardia, hypotension (80/38 mm Hg), and hypoxia. Physical examination is notable for a diastolic murmur, thready femoral pulses, and shallow breathing. The ECG shows nonspecific ST and T-wave changes. Based on the CT angiography of the chest, what is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.D: Placem ent of an intra -aortic balloon pump

question: A 63-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of diplopia, imbalance, gait difficulty, and a tendency to lean to his right. Associated symptoms include sore throat, hoarseness, dysphagia, dysarthria, and fatigue. His symptoms began immediately after a severe, sharp right-sided facial pain and have remained constant. He is right-handed but now has impaired coordination of his right arm, causing inaccuracy when bringing food to his mouth. He is afebrile and has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, he leans to the right while seated. The left pupil is 6 mm, constricting to 4 mm with light; the right pupil is 4 mm, constricting to 3 mm. There is horizontal and rotational nystagmus on right gaze with the fast phase to the left, along with mild right ptosis. The right soft palate does not elevate with phonation, and his voice is hoarse. Sensation to pain and temperature is diminished over the right face and the left trunk and extremities. Right-sided dysmetria is present on finger-to-nose testing. His gait is broad-based with leaning to the right. Based on this presentation, which of the following is the patient most likely to develop?

swallowing and talking, and fatigue during this time. He had severe stabbing pain on the right side of his face a few minutes

while walking. Based on these findings, this patient is most likely to develop which of the following?

question: A 75-year-old woman with a history of hyperthyroidism, hyperlipidemia, and coronary artery disease presents with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue and easy bruising. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin of 9.3 g/dL, hematocrit of 28%, WBC count of 1200/mm³, and platelet count of 43,000/mm³. question: A 26-year-old male medical student presents to the emergency department after being discovered by his roommate with confusion, a left-sided scalp contusion, and fresh urine on the floor approximately 30 minutes earlier. Upon arrival, his confusion resolves quickly. He recalls arriving home from class 30 minutes before being found but has no memory of subsequent events. He denies dizziness, blurred vision, fever, or nausea. Past medical history includes bacterial meningitis 5 years ago, an ACL injury from basketball 10 years ago, and unilateral renal agenesis with proteinuria. His only medication is lisinopril. He reports two recent all-night study sessions for final exams, works weekend night shifts at a fast-food restaurant, smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily for 5 years, consumes four 12-ounce beers weekly, uses cannabis three times weekly, and typically drinks 1–2 cups of coffee daily but has increased to 48 ounces daily over the past two days. Vital signs are temperature 36.9°C, pulse 88/min, respirations 16/min, blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Physical exam reveals oral blood and bite marks on the left lateral tongue; neurologic and remaining examinations are normal. Lab results show serum sodium 134 mEq/L, with other electrolytes, glucose, renal function, toxicology, and alcohol levels within normal limits. Brain MRI and EEG are unremarkable. Aside from reducing caffeine consumption, which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to prevent further seizure episodes in this patient?
original_question:

class 30 minutes before he was found. He has not had dizziness, blurred vision, fever, or nausea. Medical history is

years ago, and unilateral renal agenesis with prote inuria. His only medication is lisinopril. He stayed awake all night twice

question: A 33-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis maintained on pyridostigmine presents with a 3-day history of fever, dyspnea, and a worsening productive cough with dark yellow sputum. Her symptoms began acutely, and over-the-counter cough suppressants and acetaminophen have been ineffective. She is febrile with tachypnea and requires supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate saturation. Physical exam reveals dry mucous membranes and focal crackles and rhonchi in the left upper lung field. Chest x-ray demonstrates a patchy infiltrate in the left upper lobe. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic treatment for this patient?
original_question:

blood pressure is 138/72 mm Hg. Oxygen sat uration is 94% on oxygen at 4 L/min by nasal cannula. She  is alert and appears

for this patient at this time?

question: A 2-week-old preterm male infant born at 34 weeks' gestation is urgently assessed for reported declines in oxygen saturation and blood pressure. He has a history of respiratory distress syndrome since birth, requiring ongoing mechanical ventilation. Current ventilator settings include a PEEP of 10 cm H₂O, a rate of 50/min, a peak inspiratory pressure of 32 cm H₂O, and an FIO₂ of 0.80. His weight is 1600 g. Vital signs show a temperature of 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse 200/min, respirations 50/min, and a palpable blood pressure of 22 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 78%. Exam reveals cyanosis and poor peripheral perfusion with bilateral breath sounds. An intravenous fluid bolus has been administered. A chest x-ray obtained 15 minutes ago is available for review. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Insert ion of a central venous catheter
options.E: Placement of a left chest tube

original_question:

intravenously. Chest x -ray ordered 15  minutes ago is obtained and is shown. Which of th e following is the most appropriate 
management of this patient's condition?

question: A 14-year-old adolescent presents with a three-month history of fatigue, recurrent abdominal pain associated with loose stools, and a 7-kg (15-lb) weight loss. The abdominal pain is rated 6/10 in severity. He has also experienced two to three recurrent oral ulcers and reports mild heel discomfort with walking. He has missed significant school time due to his symptoms. His height is at the 5th percentile and his weight is at the 3rd percentile. Physical examination reveals a mildly distended abdomen with diffuse tenderness and fullness in the right lower quadrant. Vital signs and cardiac and neurological examinations are normal. Which of the following is the most likely complication of his underlying disorder?
